1:1.text: Seth. Protestants, "Sheth, Enosh, Kenan:" but in Genesis they agree with us, which shews that the translator of the two different books is different, and that there is a want of uniformity in the plan adopted by king James 1:
The posterity of Cain is neglected, as it all perished in the deluge.

1:4.text: Noe begot Sem, Cham, and Japheth. (Haydock) See Genesis 10. The author passes lightly over some of the descendants of the two latter, as he had David's genealogy principally in view.

1:6.text: But the Protestants correct it (Haydock) according to the book of Genesis and the Septuagint. The two letters are very much alike.

1:7.text: Dodanim. Hebrew has R, conformably to the Samaritan copy of Genesis, and the Septuagint translate the Rhodians. Yet Dodanim seems more accurate, (Calmet) and is retained by the Protestants.

1:10.text: Earth, first establishing the monarchy of Babylon, and building the castle.

1:17.text: Hus and Hul were the immediate sons of Aram, as well as....Mosoch, or Mes; (Genesis 10:23.; Calmet) so that there seems to be here some transposition.

1:18.text: Sale. The Roman Septuagint omits ver. 11 to 17, and ver. 18 to 24, having only, (17) "The sons of Sem, Ailam and Assur; (24) and Arphaxad, Sala."
But the other copies here insert Cainan, as the father of Sale. See Genesis 10:24.
It is a matter of great doubt whether he ought not to be inserted, Luke 3:

1:32.text: Concubine. She was his lawful wife, but of an inferior degree, and such were called concubines.
She has the title of wife, Genesis 25:1.
And the sons of Dadan, etc., seems to be copied from Genesis, as the addition is not found in many Latin manuscripts, no more than in the Hebrew or Septuagint.

1:36.text: And by. This serves to explain the difficulty; as Thamna would otherwise seem to be a daughter of Eliphaz, though we know she was his concubine, Genesis 36:12.

The Hebrew, Roman Septuagint, Syriac, and Latin, suppose that Thamna was the brother of Amalec; but the Alexandrian Septuagint has, "Now Thamna, the concubine of Eliphaz, bore Amalec." Arabic, "And Thamna, who was the concubine of Eliphaz, the son of Esau, bore him Amalec," which seems to be the true reading.
Hebrew, "And Timna and Amalek," (Protestants; Haydock) which confounds the sense.

1:38.text: Seir, not Esau, but the Horrite, (Genesis 36:20.; Menochius) which is added in order to explain the origin of Thamna.

1:40.text: Dison. We must add Oolibama, Genesis 36:25.

1:41.text: Hamram. In Genesis Hamdan. Two letters have been mistaken since the Chaldean characters have been adopted.

On this occasion, we may briefly remark, 1. The most learned fathers have admitted such mistakes in Scripture: yet these are not to be corrected by each one's private judgment, but we must all abide by the determination of the Church, which is plainly appointed for our guide in the infallible word of God. 2. To obviate the objections of infidels, respecting the apparent contradictions of Scripture, particularly in these books, we must observe that many people and places had different names; 3. And those who had the same were really distinct. 4. Frequently also grandchildren, and those who have been adopted, are mentioned as the immediate offspring. 5. Some mysterious numbers are specified, as fourteen in the genealogy of Christ, though the history allows more. 6. Odd numbers are sometimes neglected. 7. Often a part is put for the whole, or on the contrary; as Christ is said to have been dead three days, though he was only one whole day and part of two others: and in the reigns of different kings, in the same year, the different parts are assigned to each, as a whole year. 8. Sometimes two reigned together, as Joathan ruled while Ozias was still living, (4 Kings xv.) and so both reigns are sometimes counted, and, at other times, their respective years. 9. The interregnums are either omitted in calculations, or added to the years of the next ruler. 10. Only the years that a person governed well are sometimes noticed, as Saul is said to have reigned two years, (1 Kings xiii.) though his administration continued much longer. Some of these rules may be applied to most of the scriptural difficulties, as the spirit of God could not dictate any falsehood. At the same time we must be forced to acknowledge that the Scriptures are hard to be understood, 2 Peter 1:20., (Worthington) and 3:16.; and this may serve to exercise the genius, and to humble the pride of man.

1:43.text: Israel. The same remark had been made in Genesis 36:31. It is wonderful that the author of this work gives us no further information, when so many revolutions had since occurred.
But he might content himself with repeating the words of Moses.
The eight kings here specified must have reigned each 50 years, which is not impossible. David conquered the country under Adad.

1:48.text: River. Euphrates is commonly so designated. See Genesis 10:11.

1:50.text: Mezaab. It is unusual for the Scripture to mark so particularly the genealogy of a woman. We might translate the Hebrew, "a native, or who was a native of Mezaab," which is probably the same with Dizahab, "abundance of gold," Deuteronomy 1:1. Mezaab signifies, "waters of gold," (Calmet) or "whose is gold," whence some infer that the woman was very rich, (Lyranus) or had discovered the art of drawing gold thread.
It is more probably a proper name.

1:51.text: Kings. Hebrew, "Adad also died, and the dukes of Edom were duke Thamna, etc.
This, and the following names, designate the place of their residence.
The same forms of government prevailed in Idumea, as among the Hebrews, who had judges or dukes, then kings, and, after the captivity, dukes, till the time of the Machabees.

2:1.text: Israel, "seeing, or valiant with God," was before called Jacob, or "a supplanter," Genesis xxxv.

2:2.text: Aser. They are not placed in the order of their birth.

2:3.text: Her. The crime for which he was punished is not specified in Scripture.
The Rabbins say that he was so enamoured of the beauty of his wife, that he treated her in the same manner as Onan did afterwards.
It is supposed that he was slain by a devil, (Calmet) like the (Tirinus) impure husbands of Sarai, Tobias 6:14.

2:6.text: Etham. Similar names occur [in] 3 Kings 4:30. But they seem to have been different persons.

2:7.text: Achar, alias Achan, (Josue vii.; Challoner) which was his real name, as the former was given him (Calmet) in consequence of his having "troubled" Israel.
One letter may have been mistaken.
It may seem more probable that Achar, which appears invaribly in the Vatican Septuagint and Syriac, is the proper name.
Anathema, the thing devoted or accursed, viz., the spoils of Jericho.

2:10.text: Ram. He is commonly called Aram. But it is to be observed here, once for all, that it was a common thing among the Hebrews for the same person to have different names: and that it is not impossible among so many proper names, as here occur in the first nine chapters of this book, that the transcribers of the ancient Hebrew copies may have made some slips in the orthography.
Juda. Probably the first, appointed by Moses in the desert, Numbers 1:7., and 7:12.

2:11.text: Salma. Septuagint have "Salmon," as it is written [in] Ruth 4:20., and Matthew 1:4.

2:15.text: Seventh. Syriac and Arabic add, "Eliu, (chap. 27:18.) and the eighth David." It appears, in effect, the Isai had eight sons, and that David was the youngest, 1 Kings 16:10., and 17:12. The Rabbins suppose that one was only adopted, namely, Jonathan, the son of Samma, noted for his prudence and valour, 2 Kings 13:3., and 21:21.
One might die in his youth, and be therefore omitted, (Tirinus) as he had done nothing memorable.
The Scripture does not always specify the full number.

2:16.text: Sarvia. The honour shewn to the sisters of David is unusual. The father of the three valiant children of Sarvia is no where specified.

2:17.text: Ismahelite, or more correctly, "Jethra, of Jezrahel;" (2 Kings 17:25.) though the Hebrew and Septuagint in that place read "Israelite," which would be a trifling remark; and it is improbable that Isai would give his daughter to a descendant of Ismahel.
The person might, however, have resided among them.

2:18.text: He is different from Caleb, the son of Jephone.
Took, etc. Hebrew, "begot Azuba, Ischa, and Jerioth;" or rather with the Septuagint, "took to wife Gazuba and Jerioth."

Syriac and Arabic, "Caleb had Jerioth by his wife Azuba." We know that the latter was his wife, (ver. 19.) and this seems to be the true reading. As ath signifies "of or from," (Noldius) the Hebrew only requires a small correction. As it stands at present, it means, "Caleb begot Azuba, a woman, (or wife) and Jerioth." Protestants supply, "begat children of Azuba, his wife, and of Jerioth."

Her sons. The original determines us to read her, instead of "his," as ejus would as naturally be understood. If Jerioth was not the wife, she seems to have been the daughter of Caleb; as Jaser, etc., were his sons.

2:20.text: Bezeleel, the famous artist, (Exodus 31:2.; Calmet) or a different person.

The Rabbins, who confound Caleb with the son of Jephone, say that he espoused Ephrata, or Mary, the sister of Moses, when he was ten years old, that Ur and Uri had each a child at eight, and the Bezeleel began to work at the tabernacle before he was nine years old.
But this is extravagant, and the Bezeleel here mentioned was of the family of Hesron.

2:21.text: Machir, grandson of Joseph, whose descendants occupied part of Galaad, (Haydock) of which he is styled the father, or prince.
The daughter of Machir was probably an heiress, and Segub dwelt with his mother's tribe.

2:23.text: Aram. Protestants add "with the towns of Jair from them," the former possessors; (Haydock) or Gessur assisted Aram (Syria) in attacking Israel, 4 Kings 10:32.
All these villages. Protestants supply, "belonged to the sons." Septuagint, "were of the sons." All these were dependencies of Machir, "prince" of Galaad; in which sense father is taken, ver. 24.

2:24.text: Hesron. Perhaps it ought to be Azuba, ver. 19. Hebrew, "and after Hesron was dead, in Caleb-ephrata, then Abia, the wife of Hesron, bore him a (posthumous) son, Ashur, the father of those who dwelt at Thecua." He could not reside there himself no more than Caleb did at Bethlehem, which some, without proof, pretend was called Caleb-ephrata. The Septuagint agree with the Vulgate. They may signify that Caleb went to the town of Ephrata. But the son of Hebron[Hesron?] certainly never resided at Bethlehem.

2:25.text: And, is not in Hebrew. Achia, which may also signify "her sister." Septuagint, "brother." Others take (Calmet) Achia to be the first wife of Jerameel.

2:31.text: Oholai, a daughter, (ver. 34.; Tirinus) unless this son died before his father.

2:42.text: Caleb, or Calubi, ver. 9. Ziph, Maresa, and Hebron, are the names of towns, as well as of men. The descendants of Mesa inhabited Ziph, and those of Maresa dwelt at Hebron. The same remark will hold good in other places, where the names of places are put for those who occupied them.
And the sons. Hebrew, "and of the sons of Maresa."
But it may be as well explained in the sense of the Vulgate. Septuagint, "Marisa, his first-born. He was the father of Ziph, and the sons of Marisa, of the father of Hebron."
Father. Literally, "of the father," patris Hebron.

2:47.text: Jahaddai. His name occurs not before. Some suppose he was the son of Mosa: perhaps a verse may be lost, as the Syriac and Arabic pass over this and the two following verses.

2:49.text: Achsa, different from Axa, the grand-daughter of Jephone, Judges 1:12.
Both had daughters of the same name.

2:50.text: Sobal, his descendant, was prince of those who established themselves at Cariathiarim.

The Alexandrian Septuagint seems rather to assert that he was son of Hur, as well as those who follow. "The sons of Hur....Sobal....Salomon, father of Baithlammon, father of Bethleem." But the editions vary.

2:52.text: He that saw, etc. The Latin interpreter seems to have given us here, instead of the proper names, the meaning of those names in the Hebrew. He has done in like manner, ver. 55., (Challoner) and in many other places.

Hebrew, "had sons, haroe cha hamenuchoth, (Haydock) or Raia, Roeh, (chap. 4:2.) and Chazi of the canton of Menuchat, near Gabaa, 1 Paralipomenon 8:6., and Judges 20:43. Septuagint mention Manocho, Josue 15:60.

We may also translate "the father of Cariathiarim, the prince of half Manuchat, had sons who peopled different cities." It seems too harsh to call a man "half the place of rest," or "seeing from the moiety of rest," though the Hebrew has this literal signification. The Septuagint give proper names, (Calmet) "Araa, Esei, Ammanith," (but [in] ver. 55, half of Manath) and the Protestants, "Haroeh and half of the Manahattites."
Sobal left to his descendants half the country which he had quietly occupied.

2:53.text: Esthaolites. All these places were in Juda, (Calmet) or perhaps (Haydock) the two last in Dan, (Menochius) or occupied by both tribes.

2:54.text: Salma, or Salmon's descendants, peopled Bethlehem, etc.
Crowns. Valiant heroes who assisted Joab to gain crowns.

Hebrew, "Hateroth," the house (Alexandrian Septuagint, of the house of Jobab) of Joab; (Haydock) perhaps the famous general who had land near Absalom's, on the frontiers of Ephraim, where Ataroth was situated.

And half. Hebrew, "half of the Manahethites." Protestants (Haydock) or "of Menuchat, towards Zarai." The author seems purposely to mention to whom the cities at first belonged, that the right owners might be reinstated in their possessions, at their return from Babylon.
This verse may specify six towns, Bethlehem and Netophat, (Haydock) Beth-Joab, Chatsi, Manachti, and Atsothi.

2:55.text: Singing, etc. The different professions of the Rechabites are here given, instead of proper names, (Calmet) which the Vatican Septuagint retains---Thargathiim and Samathiim, Sochathim.

Hebrew Tirhathim, "porters;" (Chaldean) Shimhathim, "the obedient;" Sucathim, "the inhabitants of tents;" Calor, "the heat," as the Hebrew Chamath (Protestants, Hemath) signifies. The Cinites dwelt on the south of Juda, for which reason they are probably here mentioned, though some of them also inhabited Jabes Galaad, while the Rechabites dwelt in tents, (Haydock) and were perhaps employed as porters in the temple.

3:1.text: Hebron, during the seven years that he reigned there over Juda.
Jezrahel, a city of Juda, less noted than the one in Issachar.

3:2.text: Aggith. The mother of Adonias is styled Haggith elsewhere in the Vulgate.

3:3.text: This title refers to all the preceding.

3:5.text: Solomon is put last, though the eldest, because of his genealogy is to be continued. Some think that the three others were the sons of Urias, adopted by David, as Solomon styles himself an only son; (Proverbs 4:3.) but that only means singularly beloved, and an heir, (Calmet) as David had certainly another child by Bethsabee, who died in his infancy.
Bethsabee. Syriac and Arabic, "Bersabee." Hebrew, "Bathshuah," all erroneously.
Ammiel, or Eliam, (2 Kings 11:3.) as the same man had two names, (Calmet) or, in this instance, we must allow a transposition.

3:6.text: Hebrew substitutes Elishama and Eliphelet, and Nogah, the last name being "an evident corruption of Nepheg, and the two former being taken in too soon, and still repeated, "in their proper places." The Vatican Septuagint is likewise interpolated, (2 Kings 5:14., etc.) by the insertion of eleven names from another version, and two names are twice repeated, so that we have 24 sons of David instead of 11.

3:7.text: Noge; called Noga, (chap. 14:6.) and omitted entirely in the book of Kings. Eliphaleth being twice mentioned in this book, has made some conclude that David's first son of this name died in his youth; (Calmet) which may be said of Elisama also.

3:8.text: Eliada, or Elioda, (2 Kings 5:16.) styled Baaliada, (chap. 14:7.; Calmet) as both have the same meaning, "God's, or the Lord's knowledge."

Nine, excluding the children of Bethsabee. In 2 Kings there are only seven, or, with the sons of Bethsabee, eleven. There must be a mistake in one place, unless the book of Kings only specifies those who were then alive.

3:9.text: Some of the preceding sons were born of such, ver. 6., etc.
Thamar. She is the only one whose name is mentioned, but David had other daughters, 2 Kings 5:13.

3:15.text: Johanan, who is not mentioned in 4 Kings 23., and 24. Some think that he died young, (Tostat) or in battle with his father, against Pharao.
Joakim, or Eliacim, possessed the throne after Joachaz, or Sellum, (Calmet) which name signifies "confusion," and was given to all the sons of Josias.
Then came Joachin and Sedecias, whose proper name was Matthanias.
Joakim is passes over by St. Matthew.

3:16.text: Jechonias, who is called also Joakim, (Worthington) and Cenias in Hebrew, Jeremias 22:28.
Both the son and the grandson of Josias go under the name of Jechonias, Matthew 1:11.
Sedecias. If this be the same with the king, was born must signify succeeded, as we know that Joakim was not the father of Sedecias.

3:17.text: Asir, means a "prisoner," so that we might translate, Hebrew ,"and the sons of Jechonias, the prisoner, (at Babylon, 4 Kings 24:15.) were Salathiel."

Some think that the following were the adopted children, or successors of Jechonias; as St. Luke 3:24. insinuates that Neri was the father of Salathiel, and Jeremias (xxii. 30.) seems to declare that Jechonias should have no children. But he means such as should sit on the throne, as he explains himself, and St. Matthew (1:12.) expressly says, Jechonias begot Salathiel.

3:18.text: Sama. Septuagint, "Osamo and Nabadias." Protestants, "Hosahama," etc.

3:19.text: Phadaia, called also Salathiel, (Matthew 1:12.) or these two brothers had each a son named Zorobabel. The grandson of Salathiel was called Abiud, and that of Phadaia, Mosollam, (Menochius) unless these were the same person.
Phadaia, son of Salathiel, and father of Zorobabel, left his son to the care of his brother Salathiel, who was therefore accounted the father of Zorobabel too.

3:21.text: Son. Hebrew, "sons of Hananias, Phaltias, and Jesacias, the sons of Raphias, the sons of Arnan,...(22) the sons of Sechenias, Semeia, etc." But the Septuagint and Syriac have read in a more intelligible manner, (Calmet) intimating that these people were lineally descended from each other, (Haydock) while the Hebrew leaves us ignorant of the names of the sons of Rephaias, etc.

3:22.text: Six. Counting the father in the number.
Hebrew shisha, or "six." Sixtus V with several manuscripts, Gothic edition, etc., take it improperly for the name of a man.
Semeia and his five sons are attributed to Sechenias.
Others think that a name has been lost.

3:24.text: Oduia, the 12th from Zorobabel, which shews that this has been inserted since the time of Esdras, and that the author was very careful to preserve the genealogical tables, as it was not known from what family of the descendants of David the Messias should be born.
St. Matthew gives us a different genealogy.

4:1.text: Charmi, the great-grandson of Juda, by Zara and Zamri, Josue 7:1.

Junius takes him to be the same with Calubi, (chap. 2.) father of Sobal. This seems to be transcribed from another ancient register of the family of Juda, and designed to shew where the principal men had resided.

4:3.text: Posterity. So all the ancient versions seem to have read boni, instead of the present Hebrew abi, which literally means, "and these are the father of Etam." If words ever wanted sense, they do so here.
Etham is perhaps the name of a town.

4:7.text: Ethnan. Some would insert "and Cos," to connect this with the following.

4:8.text: Cos. Perhaps (Calmet) the same with Cenez, ver. 13.

4:9.text: There seems to be something wanting, as we are not informed who were the brethren of Jabes.
This name he received from his mother, while his father Cos, called him Othoniel.

4:10.text: For. To reward his piety and vow. Othoniel obtained Cariath-sepher, and Axa, the daugher of Caleb, Josue 15:17.
Jabes imitated holy Jacob, (Genesis xxviii.) and both desired temporal blessings, for their advancement in virtue.

4:11.text: Caleb. Hebrew, "Celub," different both from Calubi and the son of Jephone, ver. 15.
Brother, etc. Septuagint, "father of Ascha," which would seem to make him the same with the latter.

4:12.text: The latter term denotes "copper or a serpent." We know not where this city was situated, no more than Recha.

4:13.text: He was brother, or rather cousin, of Caleb. See Josue 15:17.

4:14.text: Artificers. Hebrew Charashim, which means various sorts of "workmen," (Calmet) or "father (prince.; Menochius) of Ge-Harasim, for," etc.
Under this lord the artificers dwelt who built the temple.

4:15.text: Caleb, so memorable (Calmet) for this fidelity, Numbers 12:30., and 32:12.

4:18.text: Judaia, a second wife, of his own nation. By the first, from Egypt, Mered had Miriam, etc. Some words seem to be lost, which render it doubtful to whom Judaia was married.

4:19.text: Odaia, perhaps the same with Judaia, (Calmet) as the Septuagint read.
Who was. Hebrew and Septuagint, "Machatha," (Haydock) a place to the north of Basan, far from the limits of Juda, whence we should, perhaps, read Menuchat, as 1 Paralipomenon 2:52, 54.

4:21.text: Her. Hitherto we have seem the possessions of the descendants of Phares. Those of Her inhabited the country rendered famous by the exploits of Samson with the jaw bone, Judges 15:9.
The first-born of Sela was called Her, after his unfortunate uncle, Genesis 38:7.

That wrought. Septuagint take it for a proper name, "Ebdath." Hebrew, Beth-habodath habuts, "the house of the byssus, or fine linen manufacturers in Beth-Ashbáah," (Haydock) which is, perhaps, the same with Beersabee, "the well of swearing," Genesis 21:31.
Oath. The Latin word juramenti, might be retained here, as well as Calor, 1 Paralipomenon 2:55. See 2 Kings 21:19. Septuagint read, "Esoba," as the proper name of a place.

4:22.text: He, that made, etc. viz., Joazim, the meaning of whose name in Hebrew, is, he that made the sun to stand. In like manner the following names, Lying, (Cozeba) Secure, (Joash) and Burning, (Saraph) are substituted in place of the Hebrew names of the same signification.
The first etymology is not perfectly accurate, as Jokim means simply, "he made to stand;" Jachin, the pillar which Solomon erected, has the same import.
Some injudicious person seems to have placed the sun in the margin, whence it has crept into the text.

It might be occasioned by the fable of the Rabbins, who assert (Haydock) that Elimelech wrought the like miracle as Josue, to convince the people of Bethlehem of the necessity of being converted; but, finding them incorrigible, denounced a famine, which forced him to retire with his family into the country of Moab.
Elimelech died in that country; but these four descendants of Sela retired, probably, along with him, and returned afterwards to Lehem, or Bethlehem, as it is customary to omit a part of a word.
This town was peopled by different branches of Juda's family, ver. 4., and 1 Paralipomenon 2:54.
Lahem, etc. Septuagint seem to leave the original terms: "he made them return to Abedderim Athoukiim."
Who returned, is also left as a proper name by the Protestants, (Haydock) etc. "And Jasubi Lehem."
It is not easy to decide, when the Hebrew words should be translated.
Old. Hebrew, "and ancient words;" (Haydock) records, shewing where these families resided, (Calmet) unless this be also the name (Haydock) of a place.

4:23.text: Potters. Hebrew yotserim, may also designate some family, as it seems improbable that the princes, in Moab, should be reduced to so mean a condition. But we have many examples of such a fall; (Haydock) and it is supposed, that these descendants of Sela were employed by the king of Babylon, at Nethaim and Gadera.
Hedges. These are the proper names of the places where they dwelt. In Hebrew, Netahim and Gedera.
The correct Roman edition of the Vulgate prints these words, plantationibus et Sepibus, with large letters, to imply as much.

Many of the tribe of Juda attended David, while he was forced to flee before Saul, and to hide himself in unfrequented places. Some of them might be these Yotserim, or they might follow the profession of potters.
"With the king they were powerful in his kingdom, and dwelt there."
The Vulgate often gives the meaning of proper names.

4:24.text: Jarib; perhaps the same with Sohar, (Menochius) unless the latter be Zara, or Zare, Numbers 26:12. These genealogies seem different; but the same person (Calmet) might have many names.

4:27.text: Juda, probably, not even in the following cities, which they inhabited along with them. This tribe of Simeon was always the weakest, and kept close to Juda. See Numbers 26:14.

4:30.text: Bathuel appears to be the same with Bethulia, Judith 6:7.

4:31.text: David, who had Siceleg given to him, 1 Kings 27:6.
After the schism, Juda straitened the tribe of Simeon; so that it was forced to seek for other habitations in Gador, under Ezechias, ver. 39., etc.
It could no longer reside among those of the tribe of Juda, (Du Hamel) and acknowledge another king.

4:33.text: Distribution. Hebrew and Septuagint, "and their genealogy."
Katalogismos, may as well have the former signification, "their class;" (Haydock) though the sequel seems to determine it in the latter sense.

4:34.text: Mosabab. This and the following princes joined their forces, to conquer fresh territories from Gador, (Haydock) or Gadara, near Joppe, (Strabo xvi.) which had been long in the possession (Haydock) of the Egyptians, (ver. 40.; Calmet) or Philistines.

4:40.text: Cham. It is not certain that the Philistines, who came from the country of the Casluim, were descendants of Mezraim, Genesis 10:14. But it is very clear that the Egyptians sprung from Cham, Psalm 77:51.

4:41.text: Inhabitants. Septuagint, "and the Mineans." Hebrew meyenim, "the inhabitants of Maon," in Arabia. See Judges 10:11. Syriac and Arabic, "the fountains."

4:42.text: Jesi; perhaps Asaia, by the transposition of one letter; (ver. 36.; Calmet) or these chiefs were remote descendants of Jesi, ver. 20.
The expedition probably took place about the same time as the preceding, to avoid the attack of the Assyrians, or of Juda, by retiring farther into Arabia.

4:43.text: Escape the arms of Saul, or of David.

Day. It seems, therefore, that they escaped captivity, having abandoned their own country; or this was taken from a record, which had been made before that event, and is here inserted by Esdras; though, when he wrote, these Simeonites might have experienced the fate of their brethren, who were led captives in the 6th year of Ezechias.

5:1.text: He gives this reason for not beginning with him.
And he. Protestants, "and the genealogy is not to be reckoned after the birth-right."

5:2.text: Accounted to Joseph, viz., as to the double portion, which belonged to the first-born: but the princely dignity was given to Juda, and the priesthood to Levi.
Yet it is not certain that the latter dignity formed a part of the birth-right, (Genesis 25:31.) and the author takes no notice of it.

The double portion seems to have been all the privilege, Deuteronomy 21:17. Septuagint, "the blessing or gift was Joseph's." Hebrew also does not specify princes, but "the leader," (Haydock) the Messias, (Syriac, etc.) and kings, from David till the captivity.

5:4.text: Joel. It does not appear whether he was the son of the last, or of Henoch.

5:6.text: Beera, This tribe continued to have princes of their own, after the division of the kingdom. Beera was in power when Thelgathphalnasar, or Theglathphalasar, took five tribes into captivity, 4 Kings 15:29. Only twelve generations are mentioned to fill up about 750 years, from the leaving of Egypt.
But Enoch must have lived much sooner, as the Israelites continued in that country at least 215 years. The princes are probably not all specified, as on other occasions.

5:7.text: Families, when they were led captive into Assyria; (Menochius) or before, when the tribe was in a most flourishing condition.

5:8.text: And. So Hebrew, etc., as f he was one of the princes. But the Vulgate seems to begin a new sentence, Porro, "now Bala." The possessions of this man must have been very extensive, from the Euphrates till (Haydock) Baal-meon, within nine miles of Hesebon, (Eusebius) almost (Calmet) as far as the Jordan.

Bala, or his descendants, extended their conquests as far as the Euphrates, which had been promised to Israel; and, under Saul, conquered the Agarites, who sprung from Agar. Syriac, etc., "the Arabs of Sacca," or the Scenites, "dwelling under tents," and inhabiting the Desert Arabia. The details of this war are probably given, ver. 19., etc.

5:9.text: Cattle, for which reason they desired to have this country, Numbers 32:4.

5:11.text: Selcha, not far from Jabok, Deuteronomy 3:10.

5:12.text: This list was made under Joathan, king of Juda, when Joel was at the head of the tribe of Gad.

5:15.text: And, etc. Hebrew also, "Achi, son of Abdiel."
Chief, like the preceding, each in their respective families.
They were relations (Haydock) of the same tribe.

5:16.text: Saron, in Basan; (Josue 12:18.) different from those vallies near Joppe, (Calmet) and between Thabor and Tiberias.

5:17.text: Israel. Jeroboam II had been dead two years before Joathan was born: unless some of the numbers be incorrect; which seems very probable, 4 Kings 15:5. Yet these registers might be made at different times, and one, perhaps, under Jeroboam I, as when any war seemed to threaten, the number of soldiers was to be known.
It does not appear why the king of Juda is mentioned, as the people were not under his dominion, unless (Haydock) he was contemporary with Jeroboam II, as some assert.
Yet the following expedition seems to have taken place while Israel was still faithful to God. (Haydock) By faith they overcame kingdoms, Hebrews xi.

5:19.text: But, etc. Hebrew, "and Jetur, etc., (20) and they were helped by God against them."

Septuagint, "they prevailed over them, and the Agarites," etc. The ancient, or higher Iturea, lay between Hermon and Manasses; but the lower, which Josephus includes under this name, took in the deserted possessions of that half tribe.
The former (Haydock) extended from Jordan by Libanus, to the mountains of Tyre.
And Naphis, sons of Ismael, Genesis 25:13.

5:22.text: Lord. Very dreadful, and in obedience to God's orders.

5:23.text: In Josue (xvii. 5.) Basan and Galaad seem to be given to Manasses; but the former there denotes all the level country, from the Jabok to the Jordan, as Galaad is put for the mountains on the East.
Great. It would take thirty hours to travel from the torrent Jabok to Baal Hermon.

5:26.text: Assur. These princes were scourges in the hand of God, to punish the guilty.
The former forced Israel to pay tribute; the latter carried many into captivity, 4 Kings 15:19, 29., and 16:7.
Lahela, or Hala and Haber, cities watered by the river Gozan.

6:1.text: Levi. The genealogy of the Levites is given with more exactitude than any of the preceding. Caath formed two branches, 1. the priests, descended from Aaron; 2. all his other children, who were simply Levites.
Hence they are placed after the priests.

6:3.text: Abiu. These died without children. The genealogy of Eleazar is given, as his family enjoyed the pontificate a long time, while that of Ithamar is neglected, (Calmet) as only Heli and a few others were raised to that dignity.

6:4.text: Eleazar. The nine following generations to Sadoc may be immediate.

6:5.text: Abisue. He is mentioned no where else. Josephus ([Antiquities?] V. ultra) calls him Abiezer.
Many of these high priests were very obscure.

Ozi, after whom Heli is supposed to have reigned, so that Zaraias and the three following were excluded, (Calmet) while Heli, and four of the same family of Ithamar, were acknowledged as high priests.
Abiathar was then forced to give place to Sadoc, (3 Kings 2:26.; Haydock) whose family was in power at the captivity, ver. 15.
Nicephorus (II. 4.) and Josephus ([Antiquities?] 10:11.) mention other high priests besides these.

6:9.text: Azarias. Perhaps the same who is called Amarias, (2 Paralipomenon 19:11.) as Johanan is probably the illustrious Joiada, 4 Kings 11:4.

6:10.text: The Holy Ghost gives an encomium to Joiada, for re-establishing the divine worship, (Junius) or to Azarias, for withstanding the impious attempt of king Osias. (Estius; Tirinus, etc.) (2 Paralipomenon 26:17.

6:15.text: Went out, into captivity, while his father was slain, 4 Kings 25:18. More than 22 priests would be requisite to fill up near 900 years. See Salien.

6:16.text: Gerson. Hebrew here reads Gersom, though it had Gerson before, ver. 1.

6:18.text: The, etc. This verse is repeated from ver. 2., as the author is now going to continue the genealogy of the Levites, in their natural order, (Haydock) to Samuel.

6:20.text: Zamma springs from Semei, Jeth, Gerson, and has a son named Ethan, who has Adaia; as Zara begot Athanai, ver. 41. Almost all the names are rather different

6:22.text: Asir. While Core perished, his children were preserved, Numbers 16:30., and 26:11.

6:23.text: Asir. This name seems superfluous (ver. 36., and Exodus 6:24.; Calmet) perhaps taken from the preceding verse.
The two former were sons of Core, ver. 25, 37.

6:26.text: Sophai, Nahath, and Eliab, are called Suph, Thohu, (ver. 34.; Calmet) and Eliel.
Sophai may be a surname of Elcana.

6:27.text: Elcana, his son. It seems necessary to supply, "Samuel, his son," (Haydock) as he descended from the 3d Elcana.

6:28.text: Vasseni. Some Latin copies subjoin "Joel," who was the first-born,

Vasseni means, "and the second;" which can hardly be considered as the name of the eldest son, tough some have taken it as such, in like manner as they have explained six, 1 Paralipomenon 3:22. But then the v ought at least to be omitted, (Calmet) as it is in the Vatican and Alexandrian Septuagint, "Sani and Abia."
Other editions insert "Joel," with the Syriac and Arabic.
This person had therefore either two names, or Joel has been omitted, and we should translate "Joel and the second, Abia."
These brothers acted so ill as judges, that the people took occasion to demand a king, 1 Kings 8:3.

6:31.text: Year of the World 2959, Year before Christ 1045.

The singing. Hebrew, "the hands or places of the canticle," the two tribunes on each side of the altar. Heman presided over the band in the middle, while Asaph directed those on the right, and Ethan those on the left, ver. 33, 39, and 44.

Placed. Hebrew, "rested," as before David's time it had been carried about. He erected a tabernacle for it on Sion, and directed the Levites to attend in order, (chap. 25.) so that some should be present every day (Menochius) to honour God, both by instruments and vocal music, while the priests performed their sacred functions with the utmost decency.

6:33.text: Heman. Some families among the Levites were more distinguished than the rest, having authority to regulate the singers, and to give the tune. Hence many psalms are addressed to these masters of music. Yet from those titles we cannot surely infer that those psalms were composed by Asaph, etc.

6:34.text: Eliel. Many of the genealogies do not seem to agree in these first 10 chapters, as some more obscure names have been passed over, (Menochius) or the people had different names, or, through the fault of transcribers, some alteration has taken place, which could hardly be avoided, without a constant miracle.

6:39.text: Brother, or relation, though of the family of Gerson. He also followed the same profession as Ethan did, ver. 44.

6:44.text: Ethan is also called Idithun (chap. 25:1, 8.[6.?]) in the Psalms.

6:48.text: Brethren. All the other Levites, who were not Cantors.

6:49.text: Offerings, which is here the meaning of incensum; as "incense" was not burnt on this altar, but only victims, Exodus 29:13.

6:53.text: Sadoc. By God's ordinance Heli had been appointed. But the dignity still subsisted in Aaron's family.
Achimaas, who was pontiff in the days of Solomon. This genealogy had been brought down to the Captivity, ver, 3, etc.) which shews that the author give extracts from different records.

6:55.text: Suburbs, or 2000 cubits round the city. See Josue xx., and xxi.

6:57.text: Of, is not authorized by the Vulgate civitates, but is added, to signify that all these 13 cities were not thus privileged.
There were only six cites of refuge, of which Hebron was one. See Josue 20:7.

Protestants, "And to the sons of Aaron they gave the cities of Juda, namely, Hebron, the city of refuge." But "such a licence to insert words at pleasure, cannot be allowed." Hebrew literally, "they gave cities of Hebrew refuge, Hebron," etc. The "sense absolutely disallows the word Judah," which is omitted , (Josue 21:13.) as well as in some Hebrew manuscripts in the Bamberg and Complutensian editions, and in those of Jablonski and Michaelis.
Hari, "cities," ought to be hir, "a city," as only one is meant. We should also add with the Arabic and Syriac, and the parallel passage in Josue, "and her suburbs," after Hebron.

"Juda" is not found in the Septuagint, etc.: and Buxtorf acted with little wisdom, when he thought he had sufficiently answered Capellus, by observing that the Masora declared the word to be authentic. (Anticrit.) It is a great misfortune that most of the printed Hebrew editions have been given under such influence; and that the Masorets have defaced so many manuscripts by their corrections.
Hence they probably burnt all the first edition of Naples, 1487, except part of a single copy, comprising the Cethubim given to Eton college by Dr. Pellet, which omits the word Juda.

6:59.text: Asan, perhaps Jethnan, or Ain, Josue 15:23., and 21:15. Syriac adds Ethra.
Bethsemes. Alexandrian Septuagint subjoins, "Baither and its suburbs."
All these cities were in the tribes of Juda and of Simeon.

6:60.text: Thirteen; but Geta and Gabaon are not here expressed, Josue 21:16, 17.
Alexandrian Septuagint inserts, "Labee," before, and "Anchos, with its suburbs," after Anathoth, thus making 14; but these additional cities were not sacerdotal.

6:61.text: Out of. We may supply, "the tribe of Dan, Ephraim, etc.," as only two cities belonged to Manasses, Josue 21:25.
Double that number was taken from the two other tribes.

6:65.text: Names. Some being styled of the priests, (Calmet) other of the Levites, (Menochius) whose dominion as thus asserted.

6:66.text: The cities, or rather "some cities," as all were not in the tribe of Ephraim.

6:67.text: Of the. Literally, "cities to flee to, (or of refuge) Sichem," (Haydock) which might induce some to suppose that all these cities were of this description; but, in reality, only Sichem had that privilege.
Septuagint is liable to the same ambiguity. See ver. 57., (Haydock) Josue 20:7., and 21:21.

6:68.text: Jecmaan, or Cibsaim, (Josue) as Helon is put for Aialon below. And of the tribe of Dan, Eltheco and Gabathon is omitted. See Josue 21:23.

6:70.text: Aner and Baalam, perhaps the same as Thanach and Gethremmon, which were given to the children of Caath of the inferior degree, (Josue 21:26.; Calmet) or, who were left out of the rank of priests, (Menochius) or before unprovided for, as the partition was made at different times.
Baalam. Hebrew, "Balaam." Septuagint, "Iblaam" is the Jeblaam, Josue 17:11.

6:72.text: Cedes....Ramoth, and Anem, are Cesion, Jaramoth, and Engannim, Josue 21:28, 29.

6:75.text: Hucac....Hamon, and Cariathaim, are Helcath, Hammoth-Dor, and Carthan.

6:77.text: Remmono and Thabor, or, Jecham and Cartha.
Damna and Naalol are here omitted, Josue 21:35. There was formerly a town on Thabor, perhaps called Ceselet Thabor, Josue 19:12., and Judges 4:6.

7:1.text: Jasub, or Job. See Genesis 46:13., and Numbers 26:23.
Simeron, elsewhere Samron and Semran; (Menochius) which shews, either that the names have been differently pronounced, or that the transcribers have erred.

7:2.text: Chiefs. They were at the head of distinct families.

David. We know that David took an account of his fighting men, 2 Kings xxiv. But it is not certain that this register was made at that time; as we read that Benjamin was not numbered, 1 Paralipomenon 21:6. These lists were probably made when some expedition was intended by David. The author was not, perhaps, able to recover the accounts of Nephthali, Manasses, and Ephraim, no more that the genealogies of Dan (ver. 12) and Zabulon, which do not appear in this book.

7:3.text: Izrahia. He alone deserved to be mentioned. See ver. 6, where only three sons of Benjamin appear, though there were more, 1 Paralipomenon 8:2.
Five, comprizing Israhia, "all chiefs," (Haydock) men of note, (Du Hamel) or one name may be lost.

7:4.text: Children, who had numerous offspring; (Haydock) so that, in the days of David, these descendants of Thola, amounted to so many.

7:5.text: War. In all, this tribe could muster 145,600 men, at that time.

7:6.text: Benjamin; a man, perhaps, of the tribe of Issachar. See ver. 10. Or the patriarch had originally ten sons, (Genesis 46:21.) or grandsons, five of whom only had children, and perhaps two of their families perished entirely in the war with the other tribes; (Judges 20:46.) so that, under David, only three branches were acknowledged.

7:7.text: Urai, a grandson, called Hir, ver. 12.
Five chiefs, but inferior to the seven original families, (Numbers 26:38.; Menochius) unless the fortune of war had made great changes, or some of the lists be imperfect.

7:12.text: And Hapham; called Mophim and Ophim, (Genesis xlvi.) and Suphim and Hupham, Numbers 26:39.
Aher, in Hebrew means, "another" son, called Hazim: or more probably Aher ought to be Dan, the father of Husim, (Genesis 46:23.) as Dan and Nephthali had both the same mother, Bala, ver. 13.

7:14.text: Son. Hebrew, "sons....Ezriel, whom she bare." This seems imperfect. The Hebrews seldom name the mother.
Septuagint intimate that the Syrian wife was mother of both.
Galaad, whose posterity enjoyed the country of the same name, (Numbers 22:29., and 32:41.) only Machir in mentioned as the son of Manasses.

7:15.text: And Saphan. St. Jerome seems to have taken them for women. If they were the sons of Hir, (ver. 12) Machir probably adopted them. Hebrew, "Machir took a wife from Happhim," etc. But does one woman marry two men? Syriac and Arabic, "Machir took a wife one of the sisters of Huphim and Suphim, (the great and powerful) named Maacha."
Protestants, "the sister of Huppim....whose sister's name was Maachah."
Hebrew may have this sense, with a small alteration.
Le Clerc thinks that the name of the woman has been lost, and the text altered.
Second. This also seems defective, as no first had been mentioned.
Two intervened between Machir and Salphaad, namely, Galaad and Hepher, (Numbers 26:29., and 27:1.; Menochius) so that Salphaad was his great-grandson.

7:17.text: Badan, perhaps the same with Jair; (Judges 10:3., and 1 Kings 12:11.; Calmet) or rather in this last place, the Syriac, etc., read more correctly Barac, and Samson instead of Samuel, who was then speaking. See Hebrews 11:32. There was no such deliverer as Badan, but the word has been corrupted from Barac.

7:18.text: Queen. Hebrew, "Moleceth bore Ishehod." St. Jerome and the Latin Church translate these names.
The proper names might have been as well retained, (Calmet) as in the Septuagint.
Some suppose that the mother of Josue, or Debora, may be designated.
Abiezer, (19) Leci, are the Jeser and Chelek, Numbers 26:30.

7:20.text: Bared, Thahath, probably called Becher and Theken, in Numbers. Three alone are there mentioned, though the other thirteen, whose names are given, seem to have been all the immediate sons of Ephriam, (Calmet) since he mourns for them, (ver. 22.; Menochius) and has other children afterwards.
The dreadful slaughter made of them, is perhaps the reason why only three are mentioned in Numbers, and none in Genesis.

7:21.text: Son, Ezer. Hebrew, "and Ezer and Elad." His son, after each, seems to be twice omitted, as these were in the same degree as the rest. Septuagint agree with the Vulgate.

7:21.src: Syriac and Arabic; Du Hamel; Menochius; Tirinus
7:21.text: Because they, the sons of Ephraim, (Calmet, etc.) or the men of Geth.
The text is ambiguous, but the former supposition seems more rational, (Haydock) and more generally received.

7:23.text: Beria. This name signifies, in evil, or in affliction.

7:24.text: Daughter, or great grand-daughter, repaired these three cities.
The last, probably, was called after her.

7:25.text: Thale. Hebrew, "and Thale, his son, and Thaan, his son." These seem to have been the sons of Ephraim. The following were their descendants.
At least Elisama was prince in the wilderness, (Calmet) under Moses; (Numbers 1:10.; Haydock) and Josue, the sixth from Ephraim, was 54 years old, when the Israelites left Egypt.

7:29.text: Joseph, by Ephraim and Manasses, west of the Jordan.

7:34.text: Aram. Add, "and Helem," (Calmet) unless Helem was the first-born (ver. 35) of Ahi, which signifies "brother." Perhaps Helem was brother of Somer, called Hotham, ver. 32.

7:37.text: Jethran. Septuagint, Jether," as in the following verse, ver. 38.

7:38.text: Jephone. The father of Caleb was of the tribe of Juda.

7:40.text: Of captains; a Hebrew idiom, to denote the most excellent.
Hebrew, "chief of the princes." All mentioned from ver. 30, were at the hand of their tribe, in succession, and led their brethren to battle.

8:1.text: Benjamin. Some farther particulars of his genealogy are given, for the honour of Saul; (Calmet) and many of those, who returned from captivity, are mentioned, 1 Esdras 10:11.
Bale, etc., are called Bela, (Menochius; 1 Paralipomenon 7:6.) Jadiel, and Ahiram, or Echi, Numbers 26:36., and Genesis xlvi.

8:2.text: Nohaa is probably styled Bechor; and Rapha, Ros, in Genesis.

8:6.text: Manahath; the Menuchat, in the tribe of Juda, (chap. 2:54.) after the captivity.
Abulensis thinks Manahath was in Moab, ver. 8.

8:7.text: The sentence is clearer without this particle.
Naaman was the first-born of Ahod; and Gera prevailed on his brethren to leave Gabaa, (Tirinus) and was probably the father of Oza.

8:8.text: Saharim. His father is not specified: but his was a Benjamite.
Some force the Hebrew text to say that Ahod had three sons, whom he sent to form a colony at Manahath, and afterwards he had three others by his two wives, and at last, by Rodes, he had Jobab, etc.

Begot. Protestants, "children in the country of Moab, after he had sent them away: Hushim and Baara were his wives." But the Septuagint intimate, that "Saharim begot....after he had sent away Osim, and his wife Baara, and he begot of Ada," etc.
He had perhaps retired into Moab, at the same time as Elimelech, Ruth 1:
Those who were sent away were men, (eos) according to the Hebrew, unless (Haydock) the word should be eas.

8:11.text: Mehusim. Hebrew and Septuagint, "of Husim (ver. 8) he begot."

8:12.text: Ono was three miles from Lod, or Diospolis, (Lightfoot) built after the return from Babylon, (1 Esdras 2:23.; Calmet) in the territory of Benjamin.

8:13.text: And Sama. They are supposed to have been sons of Elphaal.
Aialon belonged to Dan, Josue 19:42. But it was near Benjamin, (Menochius) and was probably occupied by people chiefly of that tribe.
Geth. We know not at what period.

8:16.text: Baria, son of Elphaal. Whether the following were his brothers does not appear certain, as it seems more natural to have placed all his father's children together, ver. 12., and 18.

8:28.text: Families. Hebrew, "These were heads of the fathers by their generations, chief men."

Jerusalem, probably after the captivity. We find none of these names from ver. 14, in the days of Josue; and it was not easy for people to establish themselves at Jerusalem, till David had expelled the Jebusites.
Yet we find that the tribes of Juda and Benjamin had seized the lower city long before, Josue 18:28.

8:29.text: Dwelt. Literally, "they dwelt." But Hebrew and Septuagint, "in Gabaon, dwelt the father of Gaboan," (Haydock) who was called Jehiel, (chap. 9:35.) or Abiel, 1 Kings 9:1.

The following verses are repeated in the next chapter, (Haydock) and seem to have been originally the same; though now many variations occur, which shew that "every Hebrew letter" has not been preserved.

8:30.text: Baal. Add, "and Ner," (ver. 33., and 1 Paralipomenon 9:36.) as the Septuagint have it here also.

8:31.text: Zacher, or Zacharias, 1 Paralipomenon 9. Hebrew omits the following name, as well as (Haydock) the end of the preceding one.
The Alexandrian Septuagint has the same faults. But the Vatican retains Macelloth.

8:32.text: Over-against, in the vicinity, while some remained at Gabaon. See Genesis 16:12.
The family of Cis resided at Gabaa, 1 Kings 10:26.

8:33.text: Ner, son of Abiel, or of Jeheil, prince of Gabaon, ver. 29.
From Aphia sprung Bechorah, Seror, Abiel, (1 Kings ix.) who is also called Ner and Jehiel. He had Ner, the father of Abner, and Cis, father of Saul.
The Hebrews avoid pronouncing the name of the idol, and substitute boseth, "confusion."
Hence it is clear that many had two names.

8:34.text: Meribbaal, alias Mephiboseth, 2 Kings 4:4., (Challoner) and 2 Kings ix.

8:40.text: Archers, particularly the natives of Gabaa, Judges 20:16. Jonathan is praised for his skill, 2 Kings 1:22.
Benjamin. We might hence conclude that here ended the genealogy of that family. Yet we find it partly repeated in the following chapter, ver. 35.

9:1.text: Israel. These registers were preserved by those who fled from the arms of Salmanasar, into the kingdom of Juda. Such records were preserved with the utmost care, and were collected by the author; who could give an account of what happened during and after the captivity, as he was an irreproachable eye-witness; not to mention the divine inspiration, which places his testimony out of the reach of criticism.
And Juda; perhaps by some historian.
Babylon, while Israel was removed elsewhere, by the Assyrians.
The genealogies of Israel being hitherto recited, now those who came back from captivity appear.

9:2.text: First. He only treats of those who returned first, (Tirinus) under Esdras.
Nehemias brought back others, and more returned afterwards. Many also retook possession of their lands, which they had enjoyed before the captivity, 1 Esdras 3:12.
Nathineans. These were the posterity of the Gabaonites, whose office was to bring wood, water, etc., for the service of the temple.

David and Solomon consigned others of the Chanaanites, whose lives they spared, to perform the same office. They were like public slaves. The number of those who returned was so small, that days were appointed for all the people to help to carry wood for the temple. At Jerusalem the Nathineans, "or people given," resided on the east side of the city; (2 Esdras 2:26.) and other towns were assigned them, 1 Esdras 2:70.
The people continued to be divided into four classes, as they had been before the captivity.

9:3.text: Manasses. People from these tribes particularly, (Haydock) as well as from others, had fled to the kingdom of Juda, when the Assyrians took their brethren prisoners; and these returned to Jerusalem.

Cyrus gave a general leave for all Israel to return. The country was too spacious for them; and it was found necessary to force the tenth part of the people, by lot, to repair to Jerusalem, as it wanted inhabitants, 2 Esdras 11:1.

9:4.text: Othei; perhaps Hathaia, son of Husias. The Hebrew names are differently pronounced.

9:5.text: Siloni may mean a native of Silo, or a descendant of Sela. See Numbers 26:20., and 2 Esdras 11:5.

9:7.text: Oduia, or Johed, or Juda, 2 Esdras xi., and 7:9.

9:11.text: High is not expressed in Pontifex; and this Azarias was only (Haydock) one of the principal families; (Menochius; Du Hamel) perhaps the second in dignity; (4 Kings 25:18.) while Jesus was the successor of Aaron, 1 Esdras 3:8.
Hebrew and Septuagint, "the ruler," (Haydock) or "captain of the guard of the house."
They were styled pontiffs, or princes of the priests.

9:12.text: Phassur, Pheshur, (Haydock) or Phetur. From him sprung Zacharias, Amsi, Phelelia, Jehoram; so that the last was only the great-grandson of Phassur, 2 Esdras 11:12.
Maasai. In Esdras 5:13 Amassai, the son of Azreel, the son of Ahazi.

9:15.text: Carpenter. Hebrew Cheresh, is taken by some to be a surname. Septuagint, "And Ares." It is asserted that the priests and Levites could follow no mechanical profession, (Abulensis) and the Bacbacar only presided over the carpenters, who wrought in the temple.

But why might not people of this order employ themselves in such arts and labours, which were never deemed degrading among the Hebrews, nor incompatible with the sacred ministry, during the leisure hours?
St. Paul did not disdain to make tents.

9:16.text: Netophati was a town of Juda. The ancient regulations could not be observed exactly.
The priests received the Levites into their cities.

9:17.text: Sellum had the superintendency over all the porters.
He was also at the head of one of the four bands which guarded the doors, east, west, north, and south of the temple, with various apartments, ver. 26.

9:18.text: Time. Hebrew, "and hitherto." This custom has been observed at the king's gate, who entered his tribune by the eastern gate of the temple, 4 Kings 16:18. So far the ancient records reach. Some would infer from this passage, that a guard was still kept at the king's gate, even though there was none sitting upon the throne after the captivity.
Protestants, ("who hitherto waited in the king's gate eastward.) They were porters in the companies of the children of Levi."
These different bands attended by turns.
But the captains were on duty every day, ver. 25, and 26, and 34.

9:19.text: Families. Hebrew, "fathers over the host, (Haydock) or camp of the Lord: keepers of the entry." The Corites, though not perhaps alone, performed what their fathers had done before the tabernacle, in the desert, and in the temple of Solomon; which were considered as the camp of the great king.

9:20.text: Before. Hebrew, "the Lord with him," an usual form of blessing, (Calmet) or of wishing well (Haydock) to the deceased.

This Phinees lived after the captivity, (Menochius) and possessed the same power as Eleazar and Joiada, 1 Paralipomenon 12:27., and Numbers 3:32. It is not necessary to refute the fables of the Rabbins, who pretend that the famous Phinees was an angel, who appeared at different times, and particularly at the return from Babylon.
Hebrew may refer to the ancient Phinees: "And Phinees....was ruler over them formerly, and the Lord was with him."

9:21.text: Gate, leading to the holy place, which corresponded with the tabernacle.

9:22.text: Towns, where they resided, when they were not on duty.
We read not of his making any such regulation. But he probably made it after the misconduct of the sons of Heli, and when the ark was brought back.

He might also give private instructions to David for the same purpose; (Haydock) as Gad and Nathan did, 2 Paralipomenon 29:25. The same plans were still adopted, though the number of Levites was smaller.

Trust, while they were at the head of affairs, and acting by God's authority. Literally, "in their faith;" (Haydock) enlightened from above, and regulating matters according to the dispositions, which they discovered in the sacred ministers: or rather Hebrew, "in their established employment," which was to subsist in future ages.

9:25.text: Up on. Hebrew, "after seven days....with them," to take their place for a week.

9:26.text: To these. Hebrew, "for these Levites, (Sellum, etc.; ver. 17) four chief porters were in their set office," for life, or as long as they were able to serve, and did not change every week like the rest.
Lord: the apartments of the priests, and the storehouses, (Haydock) where all precious ornaments, wine, etc., were kept.

9:30.text: Priests. They alone were allowed to make the composition, which no mortal was permitted to use (Calmet) for his own pleasure.

9:31.text: Pan, to be offered on the altar of holocausts, (Leviticus 2:4., and 7:12.) or to be eaten by the priests, Ezechiel 46:20. Mathathias was to take care that all this was done with decency, (Calmet) and to look after the utensils.

9:32.text: Proposition. Hebrew, "of arrangement;" (Exodus 35:13.) "of faces." These loaves were made by the Levites, but arranged upon the golden table every week (Calmet) before the ark. See 1 Paralipomenon 23:92.

9:33.text: These; probably Sellum, etc., (ver. 17) though it is not certain that the chief porters presided also over the singers, 1 Paralipomenon 15.; 1 Paralipomenon 16. Hebrew, "And these are the singers, chief of the families (Calmet; or fathers) of the Levites, in the chambers (of the temple) free" from all other avocations, (Haydock) that they may watch over the porters and musicians. Septuagint is clearer: (Calmet) "And these are the masters of music over the bands of Levites, observing their daily courses, because they are day and night over them in the works."
The sons of Caath seem to be chiefly specified, (ver. 32) though other families sung, 1 Paralipomenon 6:39.
Chambers, at a distance from their wives, to trim the lamps in the evening, and to see that none were extinguished, (Menochius) as also to teach music, etc. The Synagogues were used afterwards.

9:34.text: Jerusalem, having no substitute; (chap. 16.) (Haydock) or the chiefs of each family resided there.

9:35.text: And in. These verses are repeated from the last chapter, in order to bring us to the death of Saul, (Menochius) and the reign of David.

"Tis difficult to account for the repetition, provided they were repeated originally; but if they have been repeated through the blunder of a transcriber, the existence of the same words" before them may be the reason.
The father of Gabaon. This is the explanation of Abigabaon, 1 Paralipomenon 8:29.

Wife. So the former chapter, and all the ancient versions, with some Hebrew manuscripts, read, instead of the printed Hebrew, "sister," (Kennicott) which seems to be corrupted; (Calmet) and the Protestants venture to correct it.

9:41.text: Tharaa. Hebrew, "Tachrea." (Kennicott) e, is substituted for a.
And Ahaz, is omitted in Hebrew, (Kennicott) and Septuagint.

9:42.text: Jara. Hebrew Yahra instead of Jeuáde, or Johada, (Calmet) 1 Paralipomenon 8:36, twice. Septuagint, Jada here, and Joiada above.

9:44.text: These. All is omitted, though it is found [in] 1 Paralipomenon 8:38, and in manuscript 2:
These remarks, which may appear to some minute, shew that all Hebrew manuscripts are not absolutely alike, as some once pretended; and that the Hebrew Bible is not printed from the best copies.
"The sacred volume in Hebrew has, therefore, been published with such carelessness, as no other printed copy with which I am acquainted."
Yet this is the copy, which alone Protestants hold up as the standard of perfection, and the Rule of Faith!

10:1.text: This has no connexion with what goes before.
This history is given more at large, 1 Kings xxxi., and 2 Kings 1:

10:2.text: Year of the World 2949, Year before Christ 1055.

10:3.text: Hebrew, or "attacked him, and he feared the archers. He was not, at least, mortally wounded, when he took the desperate resolution to destroy himself, through fear and consternation. (Calmet) Yet the original will admit the sense of the Vulgate and Saul might have received some wounds. Septuagint, "Archers found him with bows, and in labours, and he laboured on account of (or to avoid) the bows."

10:4.text: Fear and reverence for his master, (Calmet) as well as concern for his own safety.

10:6.text: But, though the house of Saul was fallen from its former glory, on this fatal day, when so many of his courtiers perished; yet he left Isboseth to bewail his fate, (Haydock) and some others who were not of age to go to battle.

10:9.text: To be. Hebrew, "to carry tidings to their idols, (sorrows) and to the people." They exposed the spoils in the temples, and gave thanks to their idols for the victory.

10:10.text: Head, while his body was hung on the walls of Bethsan, 1 Kings 31:10.
The temple of Dagon was contiguous to the wall.

10:12.text: Oak, well known at Jabes. Elsewhere the word is translated, the grove.

10:13.text: See 1 Kings 13:9., and 15:23. Saul offered sacrifice unlawfully, and spared some of the Amalecites.

11:1.text: David. This name is written without i, in the books composed before the captivity, (the year before Christ 588) but i is inserted in the latter works, which is an argument against the antiquities of [Canticle of] Canticles, where the i is found. Yet the manuscripts vary so, that great stress is not laid on this (Kennicott) proof, and it is not sufficient weight to stand against the general opinion, which attributes that work to Solomon.
Hebron, after the death of Isboseth, 2 Kings 5.
Flesh; related, as the children of Jacob.

11:3.text: Which he spoke, is not expressed in Hebrew or Septuagint. Samuel complied with the injunction, by anointing David, 1 Kings 16:13. He also wrote an account of this memorable transaction, which translated the sceptre from one family to another.
It is, on this account, that the author here takes particular notice of God's decree.

11:4.text: Year of the World 2957, Year before Christ 1047.

11:5.text: Here. In 2 Kings 5:6, the passage is much fuller, and attended with many difficulties. Kennicott would correct and translate it: "And they spake unto David, saying, Thou shalt not come hither: for the blind and the lame shall keep thee off, by saying, David shall not come hither. But David took the stronghold of Sion," (different from the citadel. Josephus) "which is the city of David. And David said, on that day, whosoever (first) smiteth the Jebusites, and, through the subterraneous passage, reacheth the blind and the lame, which are hated of David's soul, because the blind and the lame continued to say, he shall not come into this house---shall be head and captain. So Joab, the son of Zeruiah, went up first, and was head."

11:8.text: Round. He made a complete inclosure or fortification, as Mello denotes perfection, or completion, (Haydock) by building houses from the castle to the town beneath it. David begun at the very house (or citadel, 4 Kings 12:20.) from which the blind and the lame thought to have excluded him, and built all round, so as to make an entire communication.

Built. Protestants, "repaired." Hebrew yechaye, (Haydock) "saved alive." (Syriac, etc.; Poole's Synop.) But probably shear is now written instead of shor, and jéje ought to be jeje. The long and the short e are easily confounded, (Haydock) and a is frequently thus inserted.
"He built....round to the beginning of that circuit. And Joab was made governor of the city."
"And surrounding it with a wall, he appointed Joab superintendant of the walls."

The position of the vowel points in these words, might naturally cause this mistake, (Haydock) as it seems to have done on other occasions. Thus shor, "an ox," should be sar, "a prince," Genesis 49:6., and Osee 12:11. Shevarim, "oxen," has been read sarim, "princes," by the Septuagint. The former passage might admit of some corrections. "In their anger they slew the men, and in their fury (vabrothom; instead of vabrotsnom, which is always explained in a good sense) they destroyed the princes. Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their fury, for it was inflexible."

11:9.text: Lord. 2 Kings adds God, more properly, as it seems always to have been inserted, though it is now wanting in two hundred places.

11:10.text: These. Besides the help which David received immediately from God, he derived great assistance from Joab, the captain-general, from the six more renowned valiant men, and from the body of Thirty, as well as from fifteen others of less note, who are mentioned here.

11:11.text: Number. 2 Kings 23:8., the names. But the two authors do not always use the same terms.
Thirty, or three, (2 Kings) more correctly.
Three. 2 Kings, eight; (Haydock) probably by mistake.

Wounded, or rather soldiers. He encountered singly a whole regiment, though he might not kill them all; much less did he attack them, when already wounded. (Kennicott) -- Numbers have often been expressed by letter in Hebrew, as well as in Greek and other languages. Our numeral figures are not liable to fewer mistakes.
Hebrew, "This is the number of the mighty men whom David had; Jashobeam, (the son of Zabdiel) the Hachmonite, chief of three. He lifted up his spear against three hundred soldiers at one time."

11:13.text: And. "Who could have discovered that thirty-four words are here omitted, if they had not been preserved in 2 Kings 23.," though with some inaccuracy? We should read, "And when the men of Israel were fled, he arose and smote the Philistines, until his hand was weary, and his hand clave unto the sword. And the Lord wrought a great deliverance that day; and the people returned after him only to spoil. And after him was Shammah, the son of Agee, the Hararite: And the Philistines were gathered together at Lechi, where was a piece of ground full of barley....14. But he placed himself in the midst of the field, (of barley) and saved it, and smote the Philistines; and the Lord wrought a great deliverance."
The name of Semma must have been omitted by the transcriber.
Without it, how will the number 37 be made out?

11:14.text: These men. Septuagint speak only of one: "And he stood," etc., (Haydock) and the verbs are singular, 2 Kings 23:12.
Gave. Hebrew vayahas (ibid.[2 Kings 23:12.?]) is more correct than vayosha, "saved."
Protestants are forced to add, "saved them by a great deliverance." But there is nothing in the original signifying by.

11:15.text: Captains. This is not rightly expressed in any of the ancient versions, no more than in the Protestant, which has, ""Three of the Thirty chief;" marginal note, "Three captains over the Thirty." The latter signification comes much nearer to the truth. It should be, "And there went down three captains, (Jesbaam, Eleazar, and Semma. Haydock) who were over Thirty." There were properly seven captains, or "heads of the other Thirty."
Rock, (hatsur) which seems preferable to katsir, "in the time of harvest," 2 Kings.

11:17.text: O that. Hebrew, "Who will." David wished to see his native country freed from the troops of the Philistines.

11:18.text: These. Hebrew, "The three (or perhaps, three of the mighty men) brake through the host of the Philistines....by the gate, and took, and brought it to David. But David would not drink it; and he poured it out unto the Lord," by way of prayer, (Kennicott) or of thanksgiving.

11:19.text: In the. Hebrew, "thing! Shall I drink the blood of these men, with their lives? for at the hazard of their lives they have brought it! And he would not drink it. These things did these three mighty men," (Kennicott) forming the first ternary.
Among these heroes there were different degrees of excellence.
Protestants acknowledge the superlative, "mightiest:" but "mighty" would do better, as they were not still equal to Joab.

11:20.text: Abisai. His name is written without the middle i, in all this book.
He was. Hebrew adds, "not;" evidently mistaking lo, "not," for lo, "ei." See 4 Kings 8:10. "He lifted up his spear against 300 soldiers, and had a name among Three."

11:21.text: Second and (Haydock) first occur not in Hebrew and Septuagint.
Hebrew, "Of the three he was more honourable than two; therefore he was their captain: and yet he attained not unto the first three."

First is indeed the sense, but it is not expressed in the original. We have here the exact order of these mighty men sufficiently marked out. Abisai is the first captain (Haydock) of the second ternary.

11:22.text: Two ariels. That is, two lions, or lion-like men; for ariel, in Hebrew, signifies "a lion (Challoner) of God," the strongest compound word for "a man of valour. The courage of a lion is so singular, that a man of extraordinary heroism is frequently called a lion, by way of emphasis; and the word God is frequently applied in Scripture to things particularly great."

V is wanting at the beginning and end of the name of Banaias, (here Benaia, instead of Ubenaiahu and Banaias, 2 Kings.) The last letter (ver. 24) serves to distinguish this third captain in waiting on the king, from the eleventh, ver. 31., and 1 Paralipomenon 27:5., and 14. (Haydock) -- Hebrew, "And Benaihu, the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man of Kabzeel, mighty in exploits; he slew two men of Moab, who were stouter than lions. He also went down and slew a lion in a pit, in a snowy day."

We have observed, that "And" is not found in the printed Hebrew nor is it in the Protestant, "Benaiah....slew two lion-like men, of Moad." Literally, "ariels;" (Septuagint) that is, "lions of God," or two of Moab equal to (Haydock) the strongest lions.
Yet it is not certain whether these were not real lions, as well as the following.
St. Jerome translates, two lions of Moab, 2 Kings 23:20.
Lion: ari is written arih, 2 Kings.

11:23.text: Cubits. 2 Kings, "a man of great aspect."
Ones. Hebrew, "and he had a name among three mighty men."
Shelosha, "three," has the u omitted, (2 Kings) as it is frequently on other occasions.

11:25.text: Among, or rather "above," as he was the second captain of this series, and not one of the body of Thirty.
Council. Literally, "placed him at his ear," (Haydock) fecit eum auricularium a secreto; (2 Kings) though the word is the same.
It seems, however, to have been read differently.
Septuagint, "he placed him over his family, (Calmet, patrian) or native place."
Syriac, "guard of his spear, and chief of his heroes." Arabic, "over all his riches." Some would translate, "captain of his guards."
Banaias was a man of consummate prudence, as well as a great warrior.
David "gives Banaias the command of his life-guard."
Hebrew, "Behold! he was more honourable than the Thirty; but he attained not unto the first three, and David set him over his guard," the Cerethites, etc., 1 Paralipomenon 18:17.

This author adopts the correction of Grotius, as the Protestants do; though he acknowledges there is no impropriety in the present reading, "over his hearing or obedience." He also adds "first," by way of explanation, as [in] ver. 21.

11:26.text: Moreover. Nothing particular is here recorded of Asahel, and therefore he is barely mentioned with the rest. But the Book of Kings had been more particular in determining their rank, and had said, "Asahel, brother of Joab, was over the Thirty," being the last of the second ternary. The preposition b (Kennicott) frequently means, "over."
Arabic, "head of Thirty, whose names are these."
Elchanan; probably the son of Jair, 2 Kings 21:18.

11:27.text: Arorite, or Harodite, (2 Kings) to distinguish him from Shammah. Eliza, who follows in 2 Kings is here universally omitted, as he is supposed to have died soon, and to have been replaced by Zabad, (ver. 41.) who completes the number of 30.

Phalonite; not Paltite, as 2 Kings, where t has been formed of the two letters un. So Septuagint have read Adni, "my lord," perhaps correctly, (Kennicott) instead of Adan, "any man's," (1 Kings 17:32.) as the two letters might easily be mistaken for one.
Helles was the captain for the seventh month, as the following was for the sixth, and Abiezer for the ninth, 1 Paralipomenon 27:10.

11:29.text: Sobbochai, rather than Mobonnai, (2 Kings) where s and m have been mistaken for m and n, which are very similar in Hebrew. The Septuagint (Aldi) has there also Sabouchai. He was the eighth captain, 1 Paralipomenon 27.
Ilai, or Selmon, 2 Kings. Septuagint have the same variation in some copies; but others lead us to suppose that Ilai was the true name. Neither occur any where else in Scripture.

11:30.text: Maharai, the tenth captain, 1 Paralipomenon 27. Heled was the twelfth.

11:31.text: Ethai. Not the famous prince of Geth, (2 Kings 15:19.; Kennicott) though the name is written alike, (2 Kings 23:29.) an i being omitted.
This captain contributed to make David king, ver. 10.
Banaia. 2 Kings xxiii., a v is added, which makes the name like that of the second general; (ver. 22.) but it ought to form part of the e, which is wanting in the following word.

11:32.text: Hurai, rather than Heddai, (2 Kings.; Kennicott) though there is some doubt which is to be preferred.
Syriac and Arabic have Hiddai; but the Septuagint declare for Hurai here, and the Aldine copy has Ouri also, 2 Kings.
Torrent. Hebrew, "of the torrents or vales of Gaas."

Abiel. 2 Kings Abialbon, which seems to be the right word, as a transcriber may omit letters more easily than he can insert them. Bun and the end seems to have been written separate, and to have been translated "the son of," by Syriac and Arabic. The particles al and al, have also been frequently mistaken on account of their nearly similar pronunciation.
Bauramite. Hebrew habacharumi, 2 Kings habarchumi, where two letters are transposed, and v is omitted, as usual, in the latter book.
The former reading appears to be more correct, as the hero probably came from Bahurim, (berim, 2 Kings 3:16.) and the Syriac and Arabic seem to have read properly.

The translator of the latter book gives the Hebrew termination throughout, instead of the Greek; (Haydock) "whence the learned have drawn an unanswerable argument against that version's being the work of the same author."
They might as well prove that St. Jerome did not translate both these books.

11:33.text: Gezonite. We know not the meaning of this epithet.

In 2 Kings, we read of the sons of Jassen, Jonathan, and Semma; (K.) but both seem to be incorrect; instead of Septuagint, "the son," or supposing m to be lost, at the beginning, "of the sons of Assem, Gouni," as Septuagint read, instead of Gezonite, which would leave this hero without any name. See 1 Paralipomenon 5:15. E is prefixed to this name, as it is to that of Manasses, Josue 1:12.

Sage, should be Shamha, (Kennicott) or Jonathan, the son of Samaa, the brother of David. He slew the monstrous giant, (chap. 20:7.) as Sabachai did another. Jonathan is mentioned also along with Asahel (2 Kings xxi.) so that he deserves a place here among David's heroes.

11:35.text: Eliphal should be Eliphelet, the son of Aasbai, the son of Machati, (Haydock) or of the country of Maacha, 4 Kings 25:23.
Eliphal and Hepher cannot constitute two heroes, as that would derange the number 37, 2 Kings 23:39. The copies of the Septuagint vary much.

11:36.text: Ahia, or rather "Eliam, the son of Achitophel, the Gilonite," 2 Kings. The name of the hero is lost here; and the latter part of his father's name (p) has also been mistaken for g, which it resembles.

11:37.text: Hesro ends with u in the Hebrew. Yet some read more properly with i, "Hetzrai," as all the ancient versions have it, except the Vulgate, (Kennicott) some copies of which read Asra.
Carmelite. Septuagint Karmedi. The D might easily be mistaken for L, as the Greek bibles (Kennicott) for some ages after Origen were written in capital letters, without accents.
Azbai seems preferable to Arbi, as the proper name seems also more correct than Pharai, 2 Kings.

The Vatican Septuagint confounds both words in one "Ouraiverchi." We may apply to many of the proper names that severe censure which St. Jerome has passed upon the transcribers of the book of Chronicles. Ita in Graecis et Latinis cod: hic nominum liber vitiosus est, ut non tam Hebraea quam Barbara quaedam et Sarmatica nomina conjecta arbitrandum eit, etc. (ep. ad Domn.) If this epistle be not genuine, the same doctor complains at least of the incorrect state of the Septuagint.

11:38.text: Joel seems better than Igaal, (2 Kings; Haydock) as we know there were some of this name [who] lived under David, (chap. 26:22., and 27:20.) and all the versions agree here, but vary much [in] 2 Kings. It is, therefore, more natural for us to adhere to that copy which has the greatest authority from the ancient versions.
Brother, relation, adopted child, (Du Hamel) or son, (2 Kings) which seems more correct, as this is an usual expression.

This reason may rather lead us to conclude that the mistake being more natural, has been made by the transcriber of that book; particularly as we no where else read of Joel being the son of Nathan, but rather of Pedaiah, etc.
The son, ought to be the proper name, "Bonni, (of Gadi, 2 Kings) or the Gadite."

11:41.text: Zabad. This name, with the following, is not found [in] 2 Kings, where Elica occurs, though not mentioned here.
We have observed that Zabad took his place, and completed the number 37, ver. 27.

11:42.text: With him. Hebrew, "above him," (Septuagint, Junius, Montanus) which must be applied to the rest of these 15 less valiant men.

Protestants agree with the Vulgate (Haydock) and Delany looks upon these 30 as a forth order of commanders in chief. But 30 with him would make 31, and why are only 15 mentioned? We have a regular gradation of officers, descending from Joab, the captain general.

These heroes are supplementary to those in the Book of Kings, and were stationed (Calmet) on the east of the Jordan, (Du Hamel) in their native country; (Calmet) which is true, at least (Haydock) with respect to several of them. Maacha, Ashtaroth, and Aroer were in these parts, (Josue 13:9, 13.; Kennicott) as well as Mathana, Numbers 21:18.

11:44.text: Jehiel. The Masorets read so in the margin; but in the text they substitute u for i, improperly. See Septuagint and 1 Paralipomenon 5:7, 8.

11:45.text: Samri. Protestants, "Shimri," or, margin, "the Shimrite," as they deem the expression to be equivalent. But Shimri was rather the father of Jedihel or Jediael, and Joha.

11:46.text: Mahumite. Protestants, "Mahavite." Kennicott would translate these last verses according to his corrections. (42) ..."Zabad, the son of Ahlai; (43) Adina, the son of Shiza, the Reubenite, and head of the Reubenites: But the thirty were his superiors. (44) Hanan, the son of Maacah; and Josaphat, the Mithnite. (45) Urzia, the Ashtarothite; Shema and Jehiel, the sons of Hotham, the Aroerite. (46) Jediel, the son of Shimri; and Joha, his brother, the Titzite. (47) Jeliel and Mahavite, and Jeribai and Joshaviah, the sons of Elnaam; and Ithmah, the Moabite; (48) Eliel, and Obed and Jashiel, the Metzobaites." It will be easily perceived that this author frequently recedes from the Protestant version, as well as from the printed Hebrew, both of which are frequently erroneous. Perhaps the critical examination of these proper names, may appear to some unimportant. But the question respecting the perfect integrity of the Hebrew text deserves to be seriously considered; and every part of sacred history requires our attention. If the Protestant rule of faith be incorrect, what security can they have? We are not exposed to the same inconvenience, even though the Vulgate be susceptible of farther improvement.

12:1.text: Year of the World 2948, Year before Christ 1056.
Siceleg, which had been given to David for a retreat. Hither many brave men came to join him.
Fled. Hebrew, "was shut up through fear."
