12:2.text: Brethren, of the same tribe. The opinion which they entertained of David, must have been very great, to make them expose themselves to such dangers. In adversity, true friendship and virtue shine the most.
The hand of providence is here conspicuous.

12:4.text: Thirty. Hebrew sheloshim, denotes "the officers" of the army.

12:6.text: Jesbaam. Probably the second after Joab, 1 Paralipomenon 11:11.
So also the Septuagint. There[They?] were Levites, descendants of Core.

12:8.text: Gaddi, a city of the tribe of Gad, (2 Kings xxiii.) from which many went to join David, when he was in the country of Moab, (Menochius) as others of the Gadites did, ver. 14.

Mountains. A fierce countenance and swiftness were much commended in soldiers. "The eyes are always first conquered." Primi in omnibus praeliis oculi vincuntur, says Tacitus, Mor. Germ. (Calmet) See Proverbs 30:30., and 2 Kings 2:18.

12:14.text: Thousand, after David had ascended the throne.
Some suppose that each of these officers was worth 100 men, etc.

12:15.text: Banks. This circumstance enhances their courage. Maundrel (Alep. p. 136) went to see the Jordan, 13th March, 1697, but the river was nine feet from the banks. Its rapidity has perhaps made its bed so much deeper, that it does not overflow so frequently at present.

12:16.text: Hold, in Moab, (1 Kings 22:4.; Du Hamel; Menochius) or to Siceleg. The people of Benjamin come with those of Juda, to avoid suspicion.

12:17.text: Hands. At that time he had done no injury to any man.

12:18.text: Spirit of God (Calmet; or good will, Du Hamel) prompted Amasai, the chief of "the officers."

12:19.text: Fight. They joined him after he had left the Philistines.
But he. Hebrew, "they helped them not." Neither David nor his partizans were involved in this danger, which might have caused their fidelity to be suspected, 1 Kings xxix.

12:20.text: Thousands: heads of the principal cities, Micheas 5:2.

12:22.text: Of God; very great, (Calmet) particularly when Isboseth was slain, 2 Kings 5:1.
The angels are to us innumerable, Job xxv. An army in concord is compared to them.

12:27.text: Joiada had the post of Eleazar over the Levites, (Numbers 3:32.) and was at the head of the warriors of his tribe, while Abiathar was high priest.

12:28.text: Of excellent. Hebrew, "of great valour." Saul had appointed him in the place of Achimelec, and David permitted him to retain his dignity.

12:29.text: Followed. Hebrew, "kept guard at the palace." These guards submitted to David, like the rest, after the death of Isboseth.

12:31.text: Manasses, west of the Jordan. See ver. 37.

12:32.text: Should, in agricultural pursuits, in which those of Issachar excelled, Genesis 49:14. Countrymen have often more skill about the weather, than the greatest astronomers, Virgil, Georg. I.
Others think that these men could calculate when the festivals would occur, (St. Jerome, Trad.) or they were well versed in politics, etc. See Esther 1:13.

12:40.text: Nephthali. All, both far and near, contributed to regale the deputies.

13:1.text: Consulted. David wished to give religion due splendour and authority, in his dominions; and he prudently contrives to obtain the general consent.
"Where there is no liberty, there is no religion;" and no change in religious matters should be attempted, without the consent of the Church.

13:2.text: To us. In the first assembly, David's motion was adopted, and a more general meeting, particularly of the priests, was convoked.

13:3.text: Sought. Protestants, "inquired not at it." Yet it is certain that many resorted to the ark, at that time. But is was not treated with such honours as David now wished.

13:5.text: Emath, from "the troubled river" Nile, (Josue 13:3.; Calmet) or Rhinocorura, (Tirinus; Menochius) to the northern extremities of the promised land, (Haydock) near Emesa.

13:6.text: Hill. Hebrew, "to Baalah," which was another name, as well as "Baalim," for Cariathiarim, 1 Kings 7:2.

13:7.text: Brother. Hebrew, "Acho," which has the same meaning.

13:11.text: Divided. Slain. See 1 Paralipomenon 14:11., 1 Kings 15:32., and 2 Kings 6:7.
The soul of brutes perishes; that of man is only divided from the body by death.

13:13.text: Obededom. A Levite was judged more proper to guard the ark, 1 Paralipomenon 15:18.
Gethite. Because he had dwelt in Geth.

14:1.text: Hiram. This embassy, and the war with the Philistines, took place before the ark was removed.

14:2.text: Perceived, by divine revelation, or by his constant success, (Menochius) as well as by the presents of "the king of Tyre, and the unanimity" of all the people.
Over his. Hebrew and Septuagint, on account of his people. Kings are more for their subjects than for themselves.

14:5.text: Eliphalet and Noga are not mentioned, 2 Kings 5:15. (Calmet) See chap 3:9.

14:7.text: Baaliada, probably the same with Eliada.

14:8.text: Year of the World 2957, Year before Christ 1047.
Seek him, with a design to oppress him, before his throne was established.

14:11.text: Baalpharasim, "the god or master of divisions."
The place was so called afterwards.

14:12.text: Burnt, conformably to Deuteronomy 7:25. These idols were commonly made of wood, and covered with plates of gold, etc. The Philistines took them along with them to battle, as the Hebrews carried the ark on many occasions. The Greeks, at the siege of Troy, had their gods on board their vessels; (Homer, Iliad ix.) and the Romans had theirs on their ensigns, in their wars.

14:14.text: Pear or mulberry trees, (2 Kings 5:23.; Calmet) on the side where the idols were.

14:15.text: Tops, or on the height. God sent an invisible army to David's assistance.

14:16.text: Gazera, on the borders of the Philistines, near Azotus.

14:17.text: All countries and nations, in the vicinity.

15:1.text: Houses, or magnificent palaces, which afterwards caused him to be ashamed, seeing the ark was lodged under skins, 2 Kings 7:10.
For it, entirely new. The old tabernacle was left at Gabaon.

15:2.text: Levites, of the family of Caath, ver. 13., and Numbers 4:5. He was rendered more cautious by the death of Oza.
For ever, as long as this law subsists.

15:8.text: Elisaphan, a Caathite, (Exodus 6:18, 22.) as well as Hebron and Oziel.
Perhaps Caath was also called by the first name.

15:12.text: Sanctified. Free from every legal uncleanness, continent, and washed.

15:13.text: Struck us. He partakes in the misfortune of Oza.
The law, through ignorance. You must attend and give proper directions.
Hebrew, "because we did not seek him with judgment," (Du Hamel) or, "after the due order."

15:17.text: Heman, Asaph, and Ethan were masters of the first band of musicians, in the reign of David. They played on cymbals of brass, ver. 19.

15:18.text: Ben. His name is omitted, ver. 20, and in the Septuagint. The Syriac, etc., have "the son of Jaziel:" but we find the 9th Psalm addressed "to Ben, chief of the band of young women," who sung and played on nables, ver. 20.
And Jaziel, called Oziel, ver. 20, as the Septuagint have here.

15:20.text: Zacharias, etc., presided over the second band, with nables, and Mathathias over the third, which had instruments of eight strings. The priests sounded the trumpets, (ver. 24.) which Chonenias directed all the music, in this solemn procession.
The Vulgate sometimes renders it psalterion, at other times lyra, (Menochius) or symphonia.
Some have supposed that the word denotes bagpipes; but it was a stringed instrument made of wood.
Hebrew, "with nebalim over halamoth," concerted things, or virgins, (Psalm 67:26.) who sung the mysterious works of God.

15:21.text: Ozaziu does not occur before. Septuagint have Ozias both here and [in] ver. 18, after Jehiel.
Of victory, such as was customary on the like occasions.
For the octave. Hebrew hasheminith, according to the Jews, means an instrument of eight strings; so when ten is mentioned, the Septuagint frequently supply "strings," Psalm 32:2.
Hebrew, "they had guitars, and presided over the eighth band to intone. All the bands formed four choirs, distinguished by their instruments, the cymbal, nable, cinnor, (or guitar) and trumpets.

15:22.text: The prophecy. Singing praises to God is here called prophecy: the more, because these singers were often inspired men.
Hebrew Massa signifies a song, (Haydock) elevation, (Calmet) burden. (Syriac) So the Vulgate has onus Moab, etc., the burden or dreadful prediction against Moab.
Prophecy often denotes no more than a melodious canticle, 1 Kings 10:10.
Chonenias directed all this music. Septuagint simply style him "prince of the Levites, prince of the musicians" (Calmet) or archon ton odon (who gave out the songs) "because he was intelligent."

15:24.text: Jehias is called Jehiel, ver. 18., and 21. He, Obededom, Barachias, and Elcana, (ver. 23.) were probably the four chief porters who attended the ark in arms; and others were, afterwards, stationed at the temple, 1 Paralipomenon 9:17. The two former took part also in the music, ver. 21.
Perhaps two preceded and two went behind the ark, to keep off the crowd.

15:26.text: Rams, in thanksgiving, that they had not been treated like Oza.
The Jews pretend that the ark was no burden to the Levites.

15:27.text: Linen. Hebrew, "a cloak of byssus," or of the finest white linen.

Byssus has often occurred before in the Vulgate, but we find the Hebrew buts, here for the first time. It probably denotes the brilliant yellow silk taken from the fish pinna, of which St. Basil speaks. (Hexa. vii.) Justinian was clothed with it, on account of its rarity and brightness. (Procopius, de fabric. Yet as it would hardly assume any colour but black, it was not so much sought after as the silk of Persia. Not only David, but all the Levites, were thus richly adorned; as the latter were at the dedication of the temple, 2 Paralipomenon 2:14. There was a manufactory of this byssus established by David, 1 Paralipomenon 4:21. That of Egypt was white, and is called schesch, or "cotton," Exodus 16:4.

Ephod. Septuagint, "a stole of byssus," or a long silken robe, (Haydock) which has been already expressed; (Tirinus) or rather, the ephod was only a girdle, which was not peculiar to the sacred ministers, 1 Kings 2:18.
Samuel, though a child, wore one.

15:29.text: Heart, and reproached him for appearing without his regal ornaments, (Haydock; 2 Kings 6:20.) as if he had been naked. No reproach could have been more unfounded, as we see David had on a robe of byssus, with the ephod.

16:1.text: Year of the World 2959, Year before Christ 1045.

16:2.text: Lord, wishing them all felicity, and making them presents, like the master of a family, ver. 43, and 2 Kings 6:18.

16:3.text: Oil. The precise signification of the Hebrew Ashisha, is not known.

16:4.text: Appointed, as a prophet, (Haydock) or by dispensation. The high priest has supreme spiritual power, Deuteronomy 17:18., etc.

16:5.text: Zacharias, at the head of the second band. These played on the nable and kinnor, as the women did not sing in the tabernacle. Jahiel should be Oziel, (chap. 15:18., etc.; Calmet) unless he be the same person, 1 Paralipomenon 15:18.

16:6.text: Jaziel. We read Eliezer, etc., 1 Paralipomenon 15:24.

16:7.text: Made. Hebrew, "gave first, to thank the Lord, into the hand of Asaph and his brethren this psalm," (Haydock) in order the 104th; and from ver. 23 we find the 95th in the Psalter, with some improvements.

16:11.text: Power. The ark was frequently the instrument of God's power; and his people are exhorted to present themselves to pray before it.
God seemed to dwell there, and often displayed his wonders.

16:22.text: Prophets. The ancient patriarchs ruled their families as kings, and directed them by word and example, like the most holy prophets.

16:27.text: Place. In Psalm 95:6., holiness and majesty in his sanctuary. Hebrew, "strength and beauty in," etc. God is served in his tabernacle with piety.

16:29.text: Beauty. Hebrew, "in the beauty of his sanctuary." Psalms, Come into his courts: adore ye the Lord in his holy court.

16:30.text: Presence. Here the psalm adds, say ye among, as [in] ver. 31.

16:35.text: And say, etc., is not in the psalm; and it seems not to agree with the flourishing state of David's kingdom: yet he might speak with the spirit of prophecy, or wish that all his subjects might live in happiness and concord under him.
Others think that Esdras inserted these verses to be sung in the second temple, (Lyranus; Estius) when many still remained in captivity. Yet they may refer to the time of Christ.

16:36.text: And sing. Hebrew, "and Alleluia," or, "all....said, (or shall say) truth and praise be to the Lord."

16:39.text: Sadoc was to minister at Gabaon, with Heman, Idithun, etc., while Abiathar, Asaph, etc., officiated at Jerusalem.

This regulation continued till towards the end of David's reign, (Haydock) when fresh orders were given, 1 Paralipomenon 23., etc. We find Abiathar and Sadoc were both at Jerusalem, when David was obliged to flee, 2 Kings 15:24., and 3 Kings 1:7. The altar at Gabaon continued some time longer, 3 Kings 3:3.

16:43.text: Bless, or feast, (Du Hamel) with his family, on part of the sacred victims.

17:1.text: Year of the World 2960, Year before Christ 1044.
Cedar. Such were esteemed the most magnificent, ver. 6. See 2 Kings vii.

17:9.text: A place permanent and secure, while I have had no fixed abode.
No more, for a long time: and unless they provoke me by their crimes, as they did afterwards, and were exposed to more severe chastisements. Constant rest is not always expedient.

17:11.text: Seed, even till Christ shall be born of the Blessed Virgin, of the family of David.

17:12.text: Ever often means only a long duration. The dominion of Christ shall have no end.
The Church founded by this true David, (Haydock) falls heir to these promises.

17:13.text: Thee. The posterity of Saul lost the throne.
If Solomon was reprobated, his children were permitted to reign. It is also presumed that he did penance; though this is not certain.

17:17.text: Men. Who was ever more favoured by God than David? Hebrew, "Thou hast regarded me according to the estate of a man of high degree."

17:18.text: Known him. Treating him with love and tenderness, as a friend. How can David sufficiently testify his gratitude?

17:21.text: Himself. Hebrew adds, "and to make thee a name."

17:27.text: Begun. Hebrew, "be pleased, (Haydock) or thou hast had the goodness to bless." Syriac, "I pray thee bless."

18:1.text: Year of the World 2960, Year before Christ 1044.
Daughters. Villages (See 2 Kings 8:1.; Menochius) and dependant towns.

18:2.text: Gifts. Hebrew mincha, a word used to denote the offerings of bread, etc., Leviticus 2:Here it signifies the tribute (Calmet) imposed on the conquered.

18:3.text: When he, the king of Soba, went to extend. Hebrew, "to strengthened his hand on the," etc.
Hand often denotes power, extent, possession.

18:4.text: Footmen. Hebrew may well signify, "1000 chariot-horses and 7000 men who mounted the chariots and the horses." We only find 1700 horsemen, 2 Kings 8:4. But soon after it is added, David houghed all the chariot-horses.
They were thus rendered useless. The king was forbidden to keep many horses.

18:5.text: Damascus. Hebrew, "Aram of Darmesek." Elsewhere the r is omitted.

18:6.text: A garrison. Literally, "soldiers." This word has been lost by the neglect of some Hebrew transcriber.

18:7.text: Quivers. Hebrew, "the shields," etc. The term is variously translated, 2 Kings.

18:8.text: Chun, called probably Betath, and Berothai, 2 Kings.

18:10.text: Desire. Hebrew, "to ask if all went well with him, and to bless him.

18:11.text: Edom, or rather Syria, (Aram) which might easily be exchanged for Edom, 2 Kings viii. David could not satisfy his devotion by building a temple; but he made preparations. Solomon left part of the treasures unconsumed, 1 Paralipomenon 26:26.
These effects, being destined for sacred uses, are said to be consecrated to the Lord.

18:12.text: Edomites. David seems to have been present, and the victory is sometimes attributed to him. Joab afterwards slew 18,000, 3 Kings 11:15., and title of Psalm ix.
Pits. This place was fatal to that nation: there Amasias destroyed 20,000, 2 Paralipomenon 25:12.

18:16.text: Abiathar and Achimelech had both names indifferently. Hebrew reads Abimelech, which is a manifest mistake. The name of Abiathar perhaps ought to be in the place of Achimelech; as we know that he was the son of the latter, and the high priest under David. Some would translate, "and Abiathar, son of Achimelech."

18:17.text: About. Literally, "at the hand of the king," to execute his orders, and to assist him. Septuagint, "vicegerents." Syriac, "princes." They are styled priests, 2 Kings 8:18.

19:1.text: Year of the World 2967, Year before Christ 1037.; 2 Kings x.
Year of the World 2968, Year before Christ 1036.

19:3.text: What evils proceed from evil counsellors!

19:4.text: From. Hebrew, "in the midst, above the thighs:" exposing them to derision.

19:6.text: Done. Hebrew, "made themselves stinking." Septuagint, "that the people of David was covered with confusion." The insult fell on all Israel.
Mesopotamia is not mentioned [in] 2 Kings, as the troops perhaps did not arrive soon enough for the first battle, ver. 16.

19:7.text: Chariots. This number seems excessive, and it may have been put for infantry; the number of which, specified [in] 2 Kings, was 33,000. But the troops of Mesopotamia are not there mentioned, and this supplies what was left defective.
The chariots may signify those who fought upon them, (Menochius) ver. 18., and Isaias 21:7.
Sometimes the whole number is specified; at others only a part.

19:16.text: River, in Mesopotamia. Joab did not allow them time to arrive.
Sophach, or Sobac. Adarezer sent also to collect these forces, 2 Kings.

19:18.text: Chariots. Literally, "chariot." 2 Kings has only 700, and 40,000 horsemen.
Ten men might be in each chariot.

19:19.text: Went. Hebrew, "made peace with David." The many petty kings who had hitherto been tributary to Adarezer, submitted now to the conqueror.

20:1.text: Year of the World 2969, Year before Christ 1035.
Year. Hebrew, "at the return of the year," in spring.

20:2.text: Melchom. Septuagint add the explanation, "their king." It may also be referred to the idol of Ammon.
The massy crown was suspended over David's head.

20:3.text: Harrows. Hebrew, "cut them with saws, and with harrows; (Haydock) or, rollers of iron, and with saws." But some other term was probably used, as we find saws before.

Protestants have "axes." This exemplary punishment was in consequence of the violation of the law of nations. Abulensis pronounced it lawful. Tirinus accuses David of a grievous sin of cruelty; observing that he was at this time involved in the sin of adultery: and people easily fall from one abyss into another. Cruelty and lust often go together. Yet it seems best to suspend our judgment.
David might only treat the most guilty in this manner.

20:4.text: Year of the World 2986, Year before Christ 1018.
Gazer, which seems preferable to Gob, 2 Kings 21:18.

20:5.text: Adeodatus. Hebrew, "Elchanan, the son of Jair, of Lehem;" or, "slew Lechemi, brother of Goliath;" or equal to him in strength.
Brother is here supplied, as it is wanting, 2 Kings.

20:7.text: Rapha, head of the giants of Palestine.

21:1.text: Year of the World 2987.; 2 Kings xxiv.

Satan. This shews that the Lord only (Haydock) permitted David's sin, (2 Kings xxiv.; Worthington) and in this sense only he is said to have instigated him; (Du Hamel) though we read this was done by the fury of the Lord, or by an evil spirit.

21:3.text: Israel, who will be severely punished.
Sin is often used in this sense.

21:5.text: The number, etc. The difference of the numbers here and [in] 2 Kings 24:9., is to be accounted for, by supposing the greater number to be that which was really found, and the lesser to be that which Joab gave in; (Challoner) or the transcribers of this place have been inaccurate.

21:6.text: Number. These might therefore escape the pestilence.

For. Hebrew, "because the king's word was abominable to Joab." We read that Benjamin was numbered unto David, 1 Paralipomenon 7:5, 11. But that might be on another occasion; or the register continued in the archives, and was not brought to the king. See 1 Paralipomenon 27:24.

21:8.text: Iniquity. David was guilty, though he retained both faith and hope.

21:9.text: Seer, or prophet, whom David kept at court and consulted. He was endued himself with the prophetic spirit.

21:10.text: Three; a word omitted in Hebrew, but supplied by the Protestants.
"I will bring three things upon thee."

21:12.text: Three years' famine; which joined with the three foregoing years of famine, mentioned [in] 2 Kings xxi., and the seventh year of the land's resting, would make up the seven years proposed by the prophet, 2 Kings 24:13.

Perhaps it would be as well to acknowledge a mistake, (2 Kings; Haydock) on account of the similitude of the Hebrew words signifying three and seven: (Tirinus) unless the prophet reduced the time from seven to three years; as in Ezechiel 4:15. God mitigates the severity of his first threat.

21:13.text: Men. Susanna spoke on a different supposition, (Daniel 13:23., and Ecclesiasticus 2:22.) of eternal punishment, in consequence of sin. David prefers to be punished by the hand of a tender father, rather than by that of an enemy; (Tirinus; Estius) and he does not wish to screen himself from suffering in this world, but offers himself to share in the chastisement of his subjects.

21:15.text: Took. Hebrew, "repented of evil," taking pity of unhappy victims.
He had been king of the Jebusites before David took Jerusalem.

21:18.text: Altar. Hence it appears that holy men erected altars by God's command.

21:20.text: Angel. Vatican Septuagint, the king and his four sons with him, along with Achabin." Other editions have, "and his four sons hidden with him." Arabic, "and the king, being come near to Aran, he saw David and his," etc. Syriac reads in like manner; but says nothing of the children either of David or of Ornan. We do not read that Ornan saw the angel, 2 Kings, but this circumstance may be here supplied. It is evident the Septuagint have read melec, "king," instead of malac, "angel."
Eupolemus says the angel Dianathan shewed David were to build the temple.

21:22.text: Worth. Hebrew, "give it me for the full price," or "worth." Septuagint, "the money was weighed."

21:25.text: Six hundred sicles, etc. This was the price of the whole place on which the temple was afterwards built: but the price of the oxen was fifty sicles of silver, 2 Kings 24:24.
Or the fifty sicles were given for the threshing-floor alone.

21:26.text: Fire, to testify his approbation. (Worthington; Tirinus) See Genesis 4:4., and 2 Paralipomenon 7:1.
This altar represented the cross, on which the Victim of our reconciliation [Jesus Christ] was offered.

21:30.text: God. He performed what God had commanded. But he would have offered other voluntary victims at Gabaon, if he had been able.
The distance shewed the propriety of building the temple at Jerusalem, (Du Hamel) which was nearly in the centre of the country.

22:1.text: The house. Or the place where the temple shall be built.
The miraculous fire convinced David that God had made choice of this spot.

22:2.text: Year of the World 2988, Year before Christ 1016.

Proselytes. This is the first time that the word occurs in the Vulgate. See Exodus 12:45. It means "strangers," (Calmet) who were not allowed to live in the country, unless they would observe the natural law, and renounce idolatry.
These had embraced the Jewish religion.

They were the remnants of the people of Chanaan, (3 Kings 9:20,) and were treated as public slaves, which could not have been done, with justice or policy, with regard to those who might barely wish to reside in the country. These strangers prefigured the Gentiles, chosen to build the Christian Church.

22:3.text: Prepared. Syriac and Arabic, "appointed blacksmiths from among the proselytes, to forge tools for cutting and dressing stone, etc." But most follow the Vulgate.
Closures is explained by the following word, which alone occurs in Hebrew, etc.
Immense. Hebrew and Septuagint, "abundance, it was not weighed."

22:4.text: Number. Still we find that Solomon ordered more, as the structure was more magnificent than even David had imagined.

22:6.text: Yet he might be 21 when he was crowned.
David began his preparations long before.
Lord. Hebrew, "must be (Haydock) for grandeur, excellence, fame, and beauty, through all countries" a sort of prodigy.

22:8.text: Blood, of Urias, (St. Jerome and the Rabbins) or rather, as David had already entertained the desire of building a temple before that event, (Estius) the blood which David had shed in just wars, must be understood; as even that causes a person to be regarded as unclean. The soldiers were obliged to be purified before they could enter the camp, Numbers 31:19. In the Christian Church, those are deemed irregular who have contributed to the death of the guilty, even as judges or witnesses. The Pagans entertained the like sentiments. (Calmet) Aeneas dares not touch the sacred vessels and household gods, when he was stained with blood, shed in his country's defence. Tu, genitor, cape sacra manu, patriosque penates Me bello e tanto digressum et caede recenti Attrectare nefas.
So. Hebrew, "much blood in my sight."

This expression enhances (Du Hamel) the greatness of the bloodshed; as when a person is said to be wicked, etc., before the Lord, it means in an extraordinary degree. The wars of David are frequently assigned as the impediment to David's building the temple, 1 Paralipomenon 17:4., and 28:3.
They would not suffer him to have sufficient leisure, ver. 18., (Haydock) 3 Kings 5:3.

22:9.text: Peaceable. Hebrew, "Solomon," which has this meaning.
Herein Solomon was a figure of Christ, who is styled the Prince of peace, Isaias ix.

22:10.text: A son. The crimes into which Solomon fell, hinder us from explaining this literally of him. St. Paul refers the expression to Jesus Christ, Hebrews 1:5.
St. Augustine (de C.[City of God?] 17:8 and 9.) observes that the promises were not perfectly fulfilled in Solomon.

22:12.text: Understanding, (sensum.) Skill to resolve difficult questions.
That. Hebrew, "and appoint thee to rule."

22:14.text: David confesses that the immense sums which he had collected, were nothing in comparison with the greatness of God. He left more than was sufficient for Solomon to perfect the work, with still greater magnificence than he had planned out, ver. 5., 1 Paralipomenon 28:2., and 29:2., etc.

Million. Josephus ([Antiquities?] 7:14.) reduces these sums to one tenth part, "of gold 10,000 talents, of silver 100,000;" so that it is "extremely probable that a cipher" was added to these numbers, in some very ancient Hebrew copy. Brerewood computes that the sum mentioned here and [in] chap 29:4, would amount to 841,125,000l. and maintains that the whole temple pavement, and all the vessels, might have been made of solid gold, without consuming it all.

"If we take the preceding talents according to bishop Cumberland's computation, the sum total will be somewhat less: but, were we to reduce it to less than one-half, would not the sum of four hundred millions of money be immense and incredible?"

A learned Jew has written this marginal note in his Bible, 1661: "It is supposed, these talents are not to be reckoned like the Mosaic, for they would amount to 720 millions. But as the Scripture makes no difference, we have no other computation to go by." See Kennicott, diss. 2:If they were the same, the sum would exceed belief. Some have thought that they were only half. Mariana supposes the talents were only the weight of sicles, or four drachms; so that David left one million for the fabric.

22:14.src: Calmet, Diss. on the riches left by David, t. ii.
22:14.text: But the relation given by historians of the riches of Sardanapalus, Cyrus, Alexander, Atabalipa, and some kings, who were not more likely to amass such treasures than David, make the account less improbable. Josephus ([Antiquities?] 7:12.) asserts, that "no prince ever left so great riches." He had extended his dominions on all sides, and imposed tribute on the conquered. He was very frugal, and had possession of the mines of Phunon, (Numbers 21:10., and 33:43.) and of Phoenicia, Deuteronomy 33:25. Though the talent seems to have varied in other nations, it always consisted of 3000 sicles among the Hebrews, at least till the captivity, Exodus 38:25, 26. We find from 2 Paralipomenon 25:6., and 4 Kings 15:19., etc., that it formed a very considerable sum. Yet Villalpand calculates that all the gold and silver left by David, would be requisite for the ornaments and vessels of the temple. If, however, we grant that it would have sufficed to build a massive temple of gold, how much must be deducted to pay the workmen? etc.
For all. Hebrew, Chaldean, Septuagint, "And to these add."
He encouraged the princes to contribute; (chap. 39.) and here he exhorts his son to shew his liberality, if any thing should be found deficient.

22:18.text: Saying is not expressed in Hebrew, "Is not the Lord," etc.
And hath. Hebrew, "for he hath given the inhabitants of the land into my hand," or power.
Almost all the neighbouring nations were subjected to David. (Calmet)--- The Lord, who assisted his people, and filled the enemy with terror.

22:19.text: Is on the point of being built. David was convinced that the work would not be much longer retarded, so that he speaks of it as present.

23:1.text: Full, satisfied, in the last year of his reign, and the 70th of his age.
Cur non ut plenus vitae conviva recedit?
Israel. This example was much followed by succeeding kings.

23:2.text: Princes, heads of families, or officers of the army, 1 Paralipomenon 25:1.

23:3.text: Numbered at the same time, when David caused his son to be crowned, and made these fresh regulations, ver. 27., and 1 Paralipomenon 26:31.

Upwards, according to the plan given by Moses. David afterwards ordered people of twenty years of age, to begin to serve in the tabernacle, as it was now fixed, and consequently the labours were diminished. This tended to augment the splendour of the religious worship, and to edify the people, who could not attend so constantly, in person.

23:4.text: Overseers. Hebrew shoterim. Septuagint, "scribes." Others suppose "lictors and executioners," who attended the judges.
These were functions from which the priests were exempted; (Calmet) so that the Levites alone are meant.
Judges. They had to decide the causes of the people, 2 Paralipomenon xxix.

23:5.text: David. Literally, "he." Septuagint, "they." Protestants, "which I made (said David) to praise therewith."

23:6.text: Into 24 courses, like the priests, that each class might serve twice a year for a whole week, 1 Paralipomenon 24:31.
God had already made choice of the posterity of Levi; and David, with the princes, Sadoc, etc., disposed of them by lots, (chap. 24:6.) to serve in the tabernacle, and in the future temple.

23:8.text: Chief, head of the rest; (Haydock) or all these three were chiefs, (Junius) no less than the sons of Semei, ver. 9.

23:9.text: The brother of Leedan had four sons, ver. 10.

23:10.text: Ziza. Hebrew has "Zina," but reads "Ziza," ver. 11.

23:11.text: Children. All together were not more than the children of each of the preceding, so that they only formed one class.

It would have been too burdensome for them to have performed the same service with those who were far more numerous: (Haydock) therefore the sons of Semei were joined with the family of Leedan: (Calmet) or rather Jaus and Baria went together, as one family.
Counted. Hebrew, "in one class, according to their father's house."

23:13.text: Minister. Hebrew, "to sanctify the holy of holies."
His. Hebrew, "in his name," with solemnity, Numbers 6:23. The Levites might bless the name of the Lord.

23:14.text: Levi, but not among the priests, (Calmet) though their father was an extraordinary priest, appointed by God to anoint Aaron, Psalm xcviii.

23:15.text: Eliezer. His being uncircumcised, had almost proved fatal to his father, Exodus 4:24.

First. He might have other brothers, (Calmet) though the title may be given to an only son, ver. 17., and Matthew 1:25. Subuel was perhaps (Haydock) chief of a family, (Calmet) and was appointed over the treasury, 1 Paralipomenon 26:24.

23:24.text: And upwards; as long as they were able to serve. They were excused from carrying the tabernacle, etc., at fifty years of age, Numbers 8:25.

To those who were turned of thirty, the higher offices were entrusted; while the Levites began at twenty, to exercise themselves in things of less consequence, under the direction of their elder brethren.
The kings might declare, by the advice of the Sanhedrim, what laws were of a temporary nature.

23:25.text: Ever. They will not have to carry, the sacred vessels about, as formerly.
In order that they may not be idle, we must therefore provide them some other employment, ver. 28, etc.

23:28.text: Place. Hebrew, "purifying all that is holy;" (Haydock) keeping clean all that is used about the sacred ministry.

23:29.text: And the priests, etc. This is not found in Hebrew or Septuagint. The Levites might prepare these things; though the priests alone were to offer them in sacrifice.
Others think that (Calmet) the priests received flour from the people, and baked it.
But St. Jerome follows the tradition of the Jews, intimating that the priests sowed the corn, and did every thing about the loaves of proposition, Malachias 1:6.
Measure. A standard was kept in the temple, (Exodus 30:13.; Calmet) to prevent any fraud. The Ediles at Rome performed the same office.
There were not two measures in the country, as that would have caused confusion.

23:30.text: And the Levites. This also is omitted in Hebrew, "and to stand," etc., as all the preceding offices pertained to the Levites, (Haydock) and priests were bound equally with them to praise the Lord.

23:31.text: Lord. Music accompanied this service twice a day, as it did the sacrifices which were offered for the prince, or for the nation, Numbers 10:10.
Rest. Hebrew, "the set festivals, by number, according to the order prescribed unto them continually," etc.

23:32.text: Brethren. Let them follow the directions of the high priests, who are of the same tribe.

24:2.text: Before. Hebrew, "in the presence of." See Leviticus 10:2.
Priesthood, under Aaron. Eleazar succeeded him, and the posterity of Ithamar obtained the dignity under Heli.

24:3.text: Ahimelech is styled also Achimelech and Abiathar, ver. 6, 21., and 1 Paralipomenon 18:16.

Courses, twenty-four, which were to serve by lot, that no one might complain, as they were equally princes, ver. 5. Hence St. Dionisius styles Zachary "prince of the priests." But the pontiff was over all.

24:4.text: Houses. The descendants of Eleazar were far more numerous.

24:5.text: Of God, great. Elohim also signifies "judges," and we might translate, "princes of the judges."
These men occupied the first dignities of the priesthood, and of the judicature, or they were princes in all sacred affairs.
They were to offer sacrifice, and to enjoy spiritual jurisdiction, in the house of God.
As their claims were therefore equal, the decision was left to lots.
It would have been otherwise difficult for all to be satisfied. See Josue 7:14.

24:6.src: Josephus, [Antiquities?] 7:2., and contra Apion i., and ii., and Luke 1:5.
24:6.text: Ithamar. All was done with the utmost order, and the priests took care to observe the regulation, till the end of the republic.

The family of Eleazar had many prerogatives over the rest, as it was longer in possession of the high priesthood, and more numerous; but that of Ithamar, gave pontiffs to the nation at the time of this regulation. The Hebrew is very obscure, and is deemed incorrect.
Protestants, "one principal household being taken for Eleazar, and one taken for Ithamar."
The insertion of "one," which is in the Septuagint, removes the difficulty.
The two families were enregistered alternately, (Menochius) till the eight families of Ithamar were done.

24:10.text: Abia, the progenitor of St. John the Baptist, Luke 1:5. Yet whether his father was "chief of the priests" of his course, as St. Dionisius asserts, or whether he sprung from Eleazar, or from Ithamar, is uncertain.

24:19.text: Aaron, and his successors. The former might have given some directions, which tradition had preserved.

24:21.text: Rohobia was in the same degree, by Eliezer.

24:23.text: And his. Protestants, "the sons of Hebron, Jeriah, the first." The words underlined, shew what omissions are in the text, (Haydock) which is probably corrupted, as well as the Septuagint. Jeria and Amarias were sons of Hebron, 1 Paralipomenon 23:19., and 26:31. Jahath may, therefore, be the same with Hebron.

24:26.text: The son. Hebrew, "the son of Jehasia, or of Oziau...." But the name of this son is lost. It is wonderful that the posterity of Gersom are passed over.
27. The sons of Merari, by Jaaziah; Beno, and Shoam."
Septuagint vary, and the original text is supposed to be changed by some transcriber, (Capel. etc.) as we read only of Moholi and Musi, ver. 26., and 1 Paralipomenon 23:21.

24:31.text: Over-against, in imitation of the priests, (Calmet) being likewise divided into twenty-four classes.
Both, etc. Hebrew, "the principal fathers over-against their younger brethren." All the rest is omitted. Lots decided the place and functions both of father and children.

25:1.text: Army; the chief magistrates of the state and of the church.
The priests, etc., were all divided into twenty-four classes, each having twelve masters to preside, ver. 7.
Idithun, or Ethan, chap 6:44., and 15:19., (Worthington) and 3 Kings 4:31.
The twenty-four sons of these three, who were chief musicians under David and Solomon, presided over the bands: eleven other inferior masters helped to instruct the rest, ver. 9.
The Jews pretend that the singers were inspired commonly, when they sounded forth God's praises in the temple, as Eliseus was, 4 Kings 3:15.
But they may be said to prophesy, because they sung the composition of the prophets.
According. Hebrew, "and the number of the workmen, (or experienced musicians) according to their service, was."
The chiefs of the twenty-four bands were carefully selected.

25:2.text: King, who sat in a separate tribune. Asaph presided in the royal city, at court, and in the tabernacle, while some of the singers were fixed at Gabaon, 1 Paralipomenon 16:7, 37, and 41.
Hebrew may signify, "at the king's command.["]
He sometimes condescended to direct the singers, ver. 6.

25:3.text: Of Idithun; or, altering the punctuation, (Du Hamel) "Now the sons of Idithun were Idithun, the first-born, Godolias (Haydock) Sori, or Isari, ver. 11. Thus, the number six, will be complete: otherwise we much acknowledge that Idithun, the father, is counted with his children; or that a name is lost, perhaps that of Semeias, (ver. 17.; Calmet) as [the] Alexandrian Septuagint has Semei before Hasabias.
Harp, (kinnor.) This was the distinctive instrument of this band, as the horn was of Heman's.

25:5.text: Seer; a title usually given to the real prophets, who foresee future things.
Gad has this title, 2 Paralipomenon 24:11. But Heman's office was to proclaim the words of God, with the sound of the horn. This musician had formerly been fixed at Gabaon, 1 Paralipomenon 16:41.
To lift up the horn, or to sing those psalms, which regarded the kingdom of David, and of Christ, as others were composed for the instruction of the people.
Daughters. These are specified, only because they joined with their brethren, in singing in the temple.
At least, we find that there were bands of young women, in the solemn procession.

25:6.text: Near. Hebrew, "under the hand of the king," who presided over a band of musicians, as well as Asaph; (Calmet) or he directed all, particularly giving his instructions to Asaph, etc.
To wit in not in Hebrew, "by the order of the king to Asaph."

25:7.text: Eight, comprising the twenty-four princes, and the eleven secondary masters of each of the bands, or twelve times twenty-four, ver. 1.

25:8.text: After the singers had been distributed into twenty-four classes, pretty equal in number, lots decided which of them should serve first, (Menochius) as had been done with the priests, 1 Paralipomenon 24:31. See 1 Paralipomenon 26:13.

25:9.text: Asaph. Add, "to him, and (Haydock) to his sons, and his brethren, twelve," who taught the rest. Unless these twelve be admitted, (Tirinus) as in the sequel, (Haydock) the number 288 will not be found, ver. 7.

Septuagint is very confused: "And the first (entire) lot of his sons and brethren came out to Asaph, of Joseph, Godolias: the second Enia, his brethren, and his sons, twelve." Hebrew, "the first lot came forth for Asaph to Joseph; the second to," etc. (Protestants) In all the subsequent verses they read, "he, his sons, and his brethren, were twelve." Why Asaph should be mentioned any more than Idithun or Heman, does not appear. Their children are placed indiscriminately by lot.

26:1.text: Porters, who guarded the temple, being stationed at the four gates. They were selected from the families of Core and Merari, (ver. 19.) and were under (Calmet) five captains, two being placed at the gate leading from the palace, ver. 16.

How many were appointed to follow the directions of these officers, we do not find. But 4,000 porters are mentioned, 1 Paralipomenon 23:5. Some guarded the treasury, etc. After the captivity, the same plan was adopted, 1 Paralipomenon 9.
Son, or descendant of Core, by Asaph, (Haydock) or Abiasaph, (chap. 6:37.) different from the musician.

26:5.text: Him. Obededom, by giving him a more numerous posterity, than any here mentioned, ver. 8. See 2 Kings 6:11.

26:6.text: Semei, who is called Semeias, ver. 4., and 7. The last syllable is not lost in Hebrew or Septuagint.
Valour. Their post was of a military nature, and required strength. Josephus says, some of the gates could hardly be shut by twenty men.

26:10.text: He had not a first-born. That is, his first-born was either dead or not fit to be chief, and therefore he made Semri the chief, (Worthington; Challoner) who was the second son (Tirinus) of Hosa. Hebrew, "for, or though he was not the first-born, yet his father made him chief." Septuagint add, "of the second division," but without reason, as they interpret, "keeping the command," instead of Semri, the chief, which leaves us without a proper name.

26:12.text: Lord. The companies succeeded each other every sabbath, but the chiefs were not changed.
The office of these men was most noble. From them were selected treasurers, overseers, prefects, etc., ver. 16-32.
The lot decided only between the heads of families.

26:14.text: Selemias: the first syllable is frequently neglected in Hebrew, 1 Paralipomenon 4:24.
Zacharias was the first-born of Meselemia, ver. 2.

26:15.text: And. Protestants, "To Obededom southward, and to his sons, the house of Asuppim."

This term may denote the "revenue;" where the offerings, destined for the support of the priests, were collected, Exodus 34:22. We find the same word used for the offerings received at the door of the temple, 2 Esdras 12:15. It may also mean the house "of the assemblies," (Calmet) where the 72 judges of the Sanhedrim met.

26:16.text: Which. Hebrew Shalecheth, (Calmet) or ssolcoth, (Haydock) "which leads or casts out" the filth from the temple.
But there were conduits for that purpose; and the meaning of the Vulgate is more accurate.
The palace lay below the tabernacle and the temple, (Tirinus) on the west.
Ward. The centinels where opposite to each other, (Menochius) or at equal distances. All were replaced at the same time.
Two captains were stationed on this road, for greater respect to the king, (Haydock) and because the concourse and dangers were increased, ver. 18.

26:17.text: Six. This gate was the most frequented, (Calmet) and the king entered by it.
Council. Hebrew asupim, as [in] ver. 15, "for the offerings two and two," (Haydock) distinct from the porters.

26:18.text: Cell. Hebrew is not clearer: "at the chamber" (Calmet; 'at the house of the instruments' for sacrifice; (Menochius) at Parbar.; Protestants) of the west, at the ascent, two at each chamber."

Protestants, "at the causey, and two at Parbar." This term is not in Hebrew. It resembles Parvar, or Pharurim, (4 Kings 23:11.) which is said to signify "suburbs," in Chaldean. But it cannot have this meaning here: and these terms are probably borrowed from the Greek, phroura, a military "post;" so that we might translate "There were at the western post, four guards for the ascent, two at each post:" one perhaps was near the temple, (Calmet) the other at the palace gates, 4 Kings 11:6. Septuagint is more diffuse, but throws no light upon this passage.

26:20.text: Now. Hebrew and Septuagint add, "the Levites:" the latter translate Achias, "their brethren."
Vessels. Or vessels of the holy places, or of things holy. Vasa sanctorum.

26:21.text: Ledan is called Leedan, (chap. 23:8.) son of Gerson. His first-born was Jahiel. This person is here styled Jehieli, and was chief of the Gersonites; at least the eldest branch.

26:22.text: Brethren. Hebrew and Septuagint, "brother." In effect, we find that Joel was the son of Leedan, 1 Paralipomenon 23.

Zathan, whose name ends in m both in Hebrew and Septuagint, may perhaps be his grandson. We often find sons in Hebrew when only one is specified. Jehieli might adopt his two brothers; so that none of his own children are mentioned.

We may also translate: "(21) These are the sons of Ledan, the Gersonite. Out of Ledan, the Gersonite, came chiefs of families: Jehiel, (22) the sons of Jehiel, Zathan and Joel, his (Jehiel's) brethren, were over," etc. It appears that Jehiel was treasurer, 1 Paralipomenon 29:8.

26:23.text: Ozielites. These are all the sons of Caath, 1 Paralipomenon 6:18.

26:24.text: Moses. Many are passed over; (Calmet) or Subael acted by his children.

26:26.text: Selemith was to guard the treasure, taken in war.
Dedicated. Literally, "sanctified," by designating for holy uses.

26:27.text: Wars. Hebrew, "out of the spoils won in battles, which they had set apart as sacred, to support the house," etc. Septuagint, "that the building of the house of the Lord might not be retarded."

Yet these spoils remained untouched by Solomon, who imitated this piety, as others did, 3 Kings 14:26. The Romans adored Jupiter Praedator, as he was styled, because part of the plunder was allotted to him. (Servius in Virgil, Aeneid iii.) Irruimus ferro et Divos ipsumque vocamus In praedam partemque Jovem. People vied with each other in the munificence of their presents.

26:28.text: Samuel, while he was at the head of affairs.
Joab. Though these three were not very religious, they concurred in promoting the splendour of the public worship, as that would at least procure them applause.

26:29.text: Them. Hebrew, "for officers (shoterim, "scribes," (Haydock) whose power was very great.; Calmet) and judges." The former are often placed first, as more honourable, so that they were not mere heralds or executioners. The administration of justice was chiefly in the hands of the Levitical tribe, 1 Paralipomenon 23:4. The sons of Hebron resided on the east, while those of Isaar and Oziel probably acted as judges on the western side of the Jordan, ver. 30.
They were not confined to the temple, but taught the people the law of Moses in the cities abroad.

26:30.text: Beyond. Hebrew meheber means, "at the passage, or on either side." Protestants, "on this side Jordan, westward." This would seem to designate the countries nearer Jerusalem.
But westward may also be understood with reference to Ruben, etc., so as to mean the parts along the river.
King. To decide both ecclesiastical and civil causes.
They also inspected the preparations for the temple, and gathered the king's tribute.

26:31.text: Jazer, a city of Gad, in Galaad.

27:1.text: Now. This list is entirely of a political nature. It seems to have been made before David had obtained the throne of Israel, (ver. 7.) though perhaps the monthly division did not comprise 24,000, before that time. This regulation was very excellent, and enabled the king to have an army of 300,000 men, at a short notice. It was not attended with any material inconvenience to the people, as none were on service above a month.
Captains. Twenty-four in number, with 240 centurions under them.
Officers. Hebrew shoterim. Septuagint, "scribes," whose employment is not well ascertained. They perhaps acted as commissaries, or kept an account of the king's troops, and reviewed them.
Septuagint, "and scribes serving the people, and the king's orders respecting the divisions, both coming in and going out, monthly, throughout the year. One division comprised 24,000 men.

27:2.text: Jesboam. Hebrew, "Jashobham," as [in] 1 Paralipomenon 11:11. He was the first after Joab, (Haydock) and a descendant of Pharez, like David, and waiting upon the king in the month of Nisan, which almost agrees with our March (Menochius) and April.

Zabdiel, the Hachmonite, may therefore denote his country; (Kennicott) or Zabdiel might be his ancestor, though the same man has frequently different names. We find several companions of this general in the sequel.

27:4.text: Dudia, or Dodi, the father of Eleazar, 1 Paralipomenon 11:12.
Hebrew, "and Macelloth, chief also of his course, consisting likewise of 24,000."

27:5.text: Priest. This we read no where else, so that some understand, "a chief officer at court," 2 Kings 8:18.
But he might be (Haydock) of the tribe of Levi.

27:6.text: Company, in his absence, (Haydock) as lieutenant.

27:7.text: After him. He acted under his father, and succeeded (Menochius) to Asahel's post, after Abner had slain him, 2 Kings 2:23., and 23:24.

27:8.text: Samaoth. Perhaps Samma, or Sammoth of Arodi, or Arari; places with which we are not acquainted, unless they denote some of his progenitors. Jazer is well known.

27:9.text: Hari. Mentioned as one of David's brave men, 1 Paralipomenon 11:28.

27:11.text: Zarahi, or of Zara, the son of Thamar, 1 Paralipomenon 11:29. Sobochai slew the giant Saph, 2 Kings 21:17.

27:13.text: Marai, or Marahai, sprung from Zara.

27:14.text: Ephraim. Pharathon was in this tribe.

27:15.text: Holdai, called Heled, the son of Baana, 1 Paralipomenon 11:30., and 2 Kings 23:29.
Gothoniel. So the Septuagint write Othoniel, (Haydock) so famous in the book of Judges, 1:13., and 3:9.

27:16.text: Chiefs, by their birth-right. The former were chosen for merit.
The tribes of Aser and of Gad are omitted, either because the list was not in the hands of the author, or it was overlooked by transcribers.
Rubenites, with whom Em Sa joins the Gadites.

27:18.text: Eliu. Probably the same with Eliab, the eldest son of Isai, (1 Kings 16:7.; Calmet) or the youngest but David, (chap. 2:15.) whose name is preserved by the Syriac, etc.

27:23.text: Heaven. It would have seemed, therefore, as if David distrusted the divine promise, (Haydock) and attempted what was impossible (Grotius) or useless.
He had before offended, and hence would now only number those who were wanted.

27:24.text: Year of the World 2987, Year before Christ 1017.
Number, those above 20; but he acted with reluctance, (Tirinus) and the wrath of heaven prevented the entire completion of the king's order, 1 Paralipomenon 21:6., and 2 Kings 24:9.

David. It would hence appear that the list was perfected, but not delivered to the king. The commissioners retained some of the lists; and, perhaps, from these, Esdras has inserted what relates to Benjamin, (chap. 7:6.) as the account was not given in 1 Paralipomenon 21:6.
This list may, however, have been made at another time; as we are informed that Benjamin and Levi were not numbered.
The Book of Kings, where we find it, is therefore a different work.

27:25.text: Treasures of gold, etc., as those of provisions had other officers.
Were in. Hebrew and Septuagint add, "the fields, in the cities," etc.
This must be understood of corn; wine is mentioned afterwards. Nothing could give us a higher opinion of David's wisdom than these details.

27:26.text: Ground, for the king. This was done at the expense of the nation, and by the people, no less than the vintage, 2 Kings 8:12. The companions of Daniel were thus appointed to superintend the workmen of Nabuchodonosor, Daniel 2:49. Xenophon (Cyrop. viii.) styles such, ergon epistatas, "men set over the works."

27:27.text: Cellars. Hebrew, "over the increase of the vineyards, for the wine cellars." (Protestants) Wine was formerly kept in cisterns, in the vineyards, till it was taken into large earthen vessels, and placed in cellars.

27:29.text: Saron, a fertile region at the foot of the mountains of Galaad; also a vale near the Mediterranean, (Calmet) in Ephraim, extending as far as Joppe.

27:30.text: Ismahelite. People of that nation deal much in camels.
Ubil had embraced the Jewish religion, or perhaps he was originally an Israelite, but had acquired the title of Ismahelite by some exploit, or by residing long in the country.

27:32.text: Man. Hebrew, "a writer." He was a good politician and lawyer.
Jahiel was perhaps the brother of Jesbaam, 1 Paralipomenon 11:11.

27:33.text: Friend. Chusai rendered the most signal service to the king, 2 Kings 16:23., and 17:1.

27:34.text: After, inferior, (Salien) as Achitophel's advice was esteemed like an oracle, (Calmet) or Joiada, a chief priest, and Abiathar, the pontiff, succeeded to his place, after he had hung himself.

28:1.text: Year of the World 2989, Year before Christ 1015.
Israel. These are more particularly described as princes of the tribes, (Haydock) twelve captains, etc., 1 Paralipomenon 27:1.
And his. Hebrew, "and of his sons."
We may also understand this of the preceptors of the royal family, 1 Paralipomenon 27:32.

28:2.text: Rising up from his bed, on which he lay, on account of his great age and weakness, while he addressed himself to his son; (3 Kings 1:47.; Calmet) or he rose from his throne, to honor this grand assembly. Hebrew, "stood upon his feet."
Thought. Hebrew, "I, with my heart, thought to," etc.
And, or which is the footstool.
The ark is often so called, because God was considered as sitting upon the wings of the cherubim, over it, Psalm 98:1., etc.
Building. No doubt what David left would have been sufficient. Yet Solomon thought that he could make something still more magnificent.

28:4.text: Ever; for a long time, and to leave the throne to my posterity for many ages, and to the Messias for ever. This expression is not always to be taken in its rigour.
This tribe had long possessed the greatest power, (Calmet) and the promise of the sceptre, Genesis xlix.
The phrase often denotes a long time, but it is best understood of Christ.

28:5.text: My son, by the mouth of Nathan, 2 Kings 7:13.
Solomon was a figure of Christ, and his dignity was predicted, 1 Paralipomenon 17:11., and 3 Kings 1:13.

28:7.text: If. The promises of the Old Testament were frequently conditional, and we always find the condition marked in some place; but those made to the Church of Christ, have no such limitation.
Day. Solomon was once faithful, and afterwards fell; it is uncertain whether ever to rise again. Hence it appears that the true children of God may become wicked.

28:8.text: Seek. Wilful (Haydock) or supine ignorance will excuse no man.

28:9.text: Know, with affection (Haydock) and faith; and him alone must thou serve.

28:11.text: Description, impressed by God on David's imagination; (Cajetan) or rather planned out by the hand of God, (ver. 19.; Salien) as the law was written.
The Jews pretend that this plan was delivered to Moses, and handed down by Josue, etc., to David.
But why might not David receive it immediately from heaven?

The original term, Ganzac, is not Hebrew. We find Ganas (Esther 3:9.) to signify a treasure, being derived from the Persian, Gaza. David gave, therefore, a description of the rooms to keep the treasures of the temple, or of the cellars for wine and oil.
Septuagint, "a pattern of the temple and of the houses thereof, and of the Zakcho, and of the upper rooms, and of the inner storehouses," etc.
Inner, or most retired closets, (3 Kings 20:30.; Calmet) where the Levites slept.
Seat, where the ark was kept, and God was rendered propitious.

28:13.text: Divisions, into twenty-four classes, (Menochius) which was done by God's order.
For all, that every thing might be done with regularity.

28:14.text: Weight, or sufficient for each, ver. 15.

Some think he only left a memorial of what quantity of gold and silver would be requisite. It does not appear that Solomon used silver for the vessels, as he chose to execute his father's injunctions with still greater magnificence.

28:15.text: Silver. David probably intended that five candlesticks would be made of this metal: but Solomon made all the ten of gold, 3 Kings 7:49. There was one in the tabernacle, Exodus 25:31.
But the temple was more spacious, and a greater number was not prohibited.
According. Hebrew, "for the use of every candlestick."

28:16.text: Silver. Yet all the ten were composed of the purest gold, (2 Paralipomenon 4:12.) as silver was too common, ibid.[2 Paralipomenon] 9:20. Moses had only two tables.

28:17.text: Lions. St. Jerome seems to have read cephir, instead of the present cephor, which is rendered "a cup, or bowl," 1 Esdras 1:10.
Alexandrian Septuagint, "and of the flesh-hooks, libation vessels and bowls; and the weight of the golden and silver vessels (kepphoure; a word which they do not translate) of each weight."
The change of i for u was very easy; and perhaps the basins were ornamented with representations of lions.
We do not find cephor used before, to denote any of the vessels of the tabernacle: but Esdras uses it, (chap. 8:27.) whence it is inferred that it is of Chaldean extraction.

28:18.text: Lord. He was represented as riding in a chariot, drawn by Cherubim, (Calmet) or sitting on their wings.
Two, particularly, filled up the space between the walls in the most holy place, and covered the ark, and also the Cherubim made by Moses, 3 Kings 7:23.

28:19.text: These things, said he, is not in Hebrew. Septuagint, "All in the handwriting of the Lord, did David give to Solomon, according to the knowledge given to him from above, respecting the workmanship of the pattern."
David saw the pattern in a kind of ecstasy; (Salien; Estius) or some prophet delivered it unto him, (Calmet) marked out by the hand of an angel, (Tirinus) like the tabernacle given to Moses.

28:20.text: Lord. Septuagint add, "and I beheld, or behold, the pattern of the temple, and of the house and the Zakcho thereof, and the upper rooms and inner storehouses, and the house of the propitiation, and the pattern of the house of the Lord: (21) and behold," ver. 11.

28:21.text: Thee. Hebrew and Septuagint, "for all workmanship, every willing skilful man for any service, also all the princes, and the people entirely at thy commands."
The willing artificers are distinguished from those who were forced to work, though the former also received pay.

29:1.text: Year of the World 2989, Year before Christ 1015.
Tender, not so much in years, for he was 22, but in comparison with David, and with reference to so great a work.

29:2.text: The dark paint used for the eyes, has the same name, 4 Kings 9:30., and Jeremias 4:30. Yet the stibium or stimmi, or alabaster, mentioned by Pliny, ([Natural History?] 33:6.) was of a sliver colour, but not transparent. The stone here specified was probably alabaster, as it was used for the pavement. There is a very fine species at Damascus, and in Arabia, which was much sought after to decorate buildings.
Chaldean has "emeralds." Septuagint, "stones of perfection, rich and various, and every precious stone, and much Parion."
But [in] Isaias 54:11, they translate the same term, "carbuncle."
The stone might resemble the agate, which is beautifully shaded with clouds and other fanciful figures.
Paros: this is taken from the Septuagint. Hebrew has simply, "and stones of ssiss."

Whether it denote the isle of Chio, or that of Chitis, in the Red Sea, the former famous for marble, and the latter for the topaz; or it may refer to Sais, a city of Egypt, which had most beautiful porphyry.
But Paros, one of the Cyclades, was most renowned for its white marble.
Josephus (Jewish Wars 6:6.) informs us, that the temple was built of large white marble stones; so that it appeared, at a distance, to be covered with snow.

29:3.text: Own. What he had already vowed, he esteemed no longer his.

Temple. Hebrew, "houses," including the various apartments belonging to the temple. The sum which David had formerly set apart out of the spoils of war, etc., amounted to 825,000,000l. What he now adds, is 16,125,000l. sterling, according to Brerewood, who deems the sums exorbitant; and others have suspected that there is a mistake in the former numbers. We have seen with what foundation, 1 Paralipomenon 22:14. David was so convinced, that the sum which he had been able to collect was too small, that he exhorted the princes to contribute, with all their power, and set them this noble example, which they endeavoured to imitate.
He had collected some of the gold of Ophir, which was esteemed the best. (Calmet) See 3 Kings 9:28.
We have before remarked, that Solomon went beyond the expectations of his father, and used no silver.

29:5.text: Fill his hand, is an expression applied to priests, by which David intimates, that any one may now offer a species of sacrifice to the Lord, Judges 7:5., and Exodus 32:29.
He wishes them to act with generosity.

29:7.text: Solids. Septuagint, "pieces of gold;" chrusous. Hebrew adarcnim, which Protestants render "drachms," (Haydock) after the Syriac, etc. Others think that the Darics, used in Persia, are meant, though they did not exist in David's time. Esdras might reduce the money to correspond with the coin with which his countrymen were then acquainted, 2 Esdras 7:70.
The Daric was equivalent to the golden sicle, which was only half the weight of one of silver, though this is not certain.
A solid was only the sixth part of an ounce, whereas the sicle weighed half an ounce, or four drachms.
Talents were always of the same weight, 125 Roman pounds.

29:8.text: Gersonite; who, with his brethren, was treasurer, 1 Paralipomenon 26:22.

29:9.text: Willingly. Their disposition was perfect: for God loves a cheerful giver, 2 Corinthians 9:7.

29:10.text: From. Septuagint, "from age to age;" (Pagnin, etc.) that is, throughout eternity: (Menochius) "for ever and ever." (Protestants) Eternity has no parts.

29:11.text: Magnificence. Thee we ought to magnify. David uses many words to express the sentiments of his grateful soul.

29:12.text: Greatness. Hebrew, "to magnify and strengthen all."
Our riches are thy gifts; and to thee we return a part, with gladness.

29:14.text: Promise. Hebrew, "to offer so willingly in this manner?"

He is astonished at the rich display of gifts: but acknowledged that all was originally sent by God. In the same sentiments, we say in the mass, "We offer unto thee of thy own presents and gifts;" or, as the Greek expresses it, ta sa apo ton son.

29:15.text: Strangers. We have nothing but what we have received from thee; and for how short a time!
No stay. Hebrew, "none abiding, (Haydock; or) no hope" of being able to escape death, (Calmet) when we must leave all. How happy, therefore, are those who sent their treasures before them!
All are pilgrims, with respect to heaven, Hebrews xiii.

29:17.text: Simplicity. Hebrew, "uprightness." Septuagint, "justice;" (Haydock) a pure intention, which our Saviour styled a single eye, Matthew 6:22.

29:18.text: This. Hebrew, "keep this for ever, in the imagination of the thoughts of the heart of thy people, and direct their heart unto thee. Preserve these good dispositions, which thou hast given them."

29:20.text: Then is not in Hebrew or Septuagint.
The same term is used, to express the outward adoration which they shewed to God, and the civil respect which was due to the king: pari gestu, says Grotius, animo diverso.

How then will Protestants prove that we are guilty of idolatry, when we bow down before the cross, etc., unless they pretend to know the secrets of hearts? Protestants, they "bowed down their heads, (Septuagint, knees) and worshipped the Lord and the king."
The exterior set was the same, but the intention determined the application. See Exodus xx.

29:21.text: And with. Hebrew and Septuagint, "and their sacrifices of wine and victims, (or peace-offerings, to be eaten by the people.; Calmet) in abundance for all Israel."

29:22.text: The Lord, at Jerusalem, (Menochius) where the ark was then kept.

Second time: the first had been done with too much precipitation, in consequence of the attempt of Adonias; (3 Kings 1:39.; Tirinus) or this took place after the death of David, that his successor might be invested with full power, (Grotius) and be acknowledged by all.

Priest. This at least only took place after the death of David, when Abiathar fell into disgrace, 3 Kings 2:35. Both prince and priest must act by God's authority; and those who resist them, resist God himself, Romans 13:1.
They are ministers of the Lord.

29:24.text: Gave. Hebrew, "placed their hands upon Solomon." Septuagint, "were subject to him."
The latter words in the Vulgate explain the meaning of the ceremony.

It seems to have been similar to that used when Abraham required an oath of his servant, Genesis 24:2. Vassals placed their hands within those of their Lord, under whom they hold lands; (Calmet) and the descendants of the Germans testify their submission, by putting their hands between a person's knees.
The nobles took the oath of fidelity to Solomon, by some such method.

29:28.text: Year of the World 2990, Year before Christ 1014.

29:29.text: Gad, the seer "of David," as he is sometimes styled. These three were well acquainted with David, (Calmet) and wrote the two first books of Kings; (Haydock) or at least those books are compiled from their memorials, (Calmet) if their works be lost, (Menochius) which is uncertain.

29:30.text: Under him in various tribulations, towards the end of his reign.
Those prophets recorded not only what regarded David, (Haydock) but also what happened of consequence, in other nations, with which he had any connections.
Of the. Syriac and Arabic, "of his land, or among the kings of his race."
David reduced under his dominion not only the nations which dwelt in Chanaan, but all those which had been promised to Israel.
